concepts.a: They treat symptom more than root food-environment causes, with risks like muscle loss, GI effects, and unknown long-term impacts. Pharma incentives favor chronic disease management over fixing the ultra-processed food supply.
concepts.definition: GLP-1 analogues (semaglutide/Ozempic/Wegovy; tirzepatide/Mounjaro/Zepbound) are effective for weight loss and diabetes — but robert lustig urges caution: they treat symptom not cause, carry real risks, and align with Big Pharma incentives to manage chronic disease rather than fix the food supply.

concepts.title: GLP-1 Substance Use Treatment
concepts.q: How might GLP-1 agonists help people with alcohol problems beyond weight loss?
concepts.a: They may reduce substance craving—especially alcohol—via satiety-like mechanisms, producing the 'I want not to want' effect. Drinking often falls as a side benefit when overweight patients take GLP-1 for weight.
concepts.definition: GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide/Wegovy/Ozempic) may reduce substance craving — especially alcohol (most "eating-like" drug behavior; satiety mechanism). Pattern in animal studies, small trials, epidemiology: alcohol use/ER visits drop on semaglutide. Patient desire: "I want not to want" — GLP-1 silences all-day "don't eat" effort (Humphreys friend anecdote). Trojan-horse clinic model: overweight patients take GLP-1 for weight; drinking falls as side benefit (Anna Lembke patients). Humphreys working with VA + Novo + philanthropist on trials. ~70% of problem drinkers overweight — dual benefit potential. 20-year drug safety database reduces long-latency surprise worry. Not universally positive in a…

concepts.title: Glucose-Ketone Index (GKI)
concepts.q: What does a lower glucose-to-ketone blood ratio indicate about metabolic fuel state relevant to tumor cells?
concepts.a: Lower GKI (low glucose plus elevated nutritional ketones) marks terrain where tumor cells lose a primary fuel while healthy cells can thrive on ketones. It differs from diabetic ketoacidosis, which has high glucose and high ketones.
concepts.definition: Biomarker ratio of blood glucose to ketones developed by thomas seyfried as a practical readout of mitochondrial fuel state. Lower GKI (low glucose + elevated nutritional ketones) marks the metabolic terrain where tumor cells lose a primary fuel while healthy cells thrive on ketones. Distinguished from diabetic ketoacidosis (high glucose and high ketones).

concepts.title: Glucose Visual Proximity Illusion
concepts.q: In a blind sugar-vs-Splenda drink study, how did real caloric energy change perception of a finish line?
concepts.a: The sugar group perceived space as more constricted—the finish line looked closer—while Splenda stayed at baseline. When the body has more available energy, goals look easier; distances feel less far.
concepts.definition: emily balcetis lab experiment: participants drank Kool-Aid sweetened with either real sugar (caloric energy) or Splenda (sweet taste, no calories). Noxious Kool-Aid powder masked taste — participants couldn't guess which they received (verified by post-test guessing + blood glucose measurement). ~10–15 min for sugar to metabolize; researcher also blind to condition. Result: Sugar group perceived space as more constricted — finish line in an exercise task appeared closer. Splenda group stayed at baseline perception. Interpretation: when the body has more available energy, the world looks easier — distances don't look as far. Mirrors chronic-fatigue / overweight perception findings in fitness …

concepts.title: Glymphatic Sleep Cleansing
concepts.q: What happens to brain waste clearance during sleep, and roughly how much actual sleep does a full cycle need?
concepts.a: The glymphatic system flushes toxins like tau and amyloid as perivascular space expands (~60%) and CSF clears waste. A full cleaning cycle needs about 7–8 hours of actual sleep, not just time in bed.
concepts.definition: The glymphatic system (glial + lymphatic) actively flushes brain toxins during sleep — jets of fluid remove tau proteins, amyloid plaques, and neurofibrillary tangles linked to Alzheimer's and Parkinson's pathology. The full cleaning cycle takes 7–8 hours of actual sleep (not time in bed awake). Mechanism (improve lymphatic system health appearance huberman): Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) bathes the brain. During sleep, perivascular space around brain vasculature expands ~60% — astrocyte "end feet" push outward and express aquaporin-4 channels (circadian-regulated; Nobel for discovery). CSF waste flows along vasculature, exits via cranial nerve pathways, joins systemic lymphatic/blood clearance.…

concepts.title: Glyphosate Cell Communication Disruption
concepts.q: According to Zach Bush's lab thesis, how does widespread glyphosate exposure supposedly harm human cell systems?
concepts.a: It dissolves communication between cell systems by blurring cellular boundaries, so the immune system fights 'everything' and cells leak. The herbicide (also patented as antibiotic/antifungal) is tied to chronic-disease rise after Roundup Ready crops.
concepts.definition: zach bush lab thesis: glyphosate (primary herbicide in ~90%+ weed killers; ~4B lb sprayed into soil/water worldwide) dissolves communication between human cell systems — blurs cellular boundaries so the immune system fights "everything," and cells leak crystal water/light. Patented as antibiotic/antifungal/antiparasite; Roundup Ready seeds = poison-tolerant crops that gut microbiomes are not ready for. Tied to the 1992–2002 Western chronic-disease collapse across ages/organs.

concepts.title: Goal Command
concepts.q: In Claude Code's /goal workflow, why must a separate evaluator model grade progress instead of letting the worker agent declare done?
concepts.a: Separating worker from judge prevents sycophantic self-declaration of completion. The agent keeps working until objective, measurable done-criteria are met as graded by a different persona.
concepts.definition: Claude Code's /goal command sets an objective completion condition and keeps working turn-after-turn until met. Key design: a separate evaluator model (different persona) grades each turn — the worker agent cannot declare itself done. Separates worker from judge, addressing the same sycophancy problem as roast skill. UI shows "goal active" with elapsed time; completes with "goal done." Best practices from claude code make money four upgrades: - Define objective, measurable done-criteria (file counts, minimum competitors, draft counts) - Embed parallel sub-agents inside the goal prompt (one per deliverable, non-overwriting files) - Include a verification pass after sub-agents finish ("open ea…

concepts.title: Goal Pursuit Neural Circuits
concepts.q: Why do fitness, career, and relationship goals recruit overlapping brain systems rather than totally separate circuits?
concepts.a: All goal types share value-assessment and action-selection architecture—amygdala avoidance, basal ganglia go/no-go, lateral PFC planning, orbitofrontal progress emotion—with dopamine as common currency for pursuit value and progress.
concepts.definition: All goal types — fitness, career, relationships, finance — recruit a shared neural architecture divided into value assessment and action selection. | Region | Role | Simple label | |---|---|---| | Amygdala | Fear/anxiety; avoidance of punishment (embarrassment, ruin) | Avoidance value | | Basal ganglia (ventral striatum) | Go circuits (initiate) + no-go circuits (inhibit) | Action / inaction | | Lateral prefrontal cortex | Executive planning across time scales | Future planning | | Orbitofrontal cortex | Current emotional progress vs anticipated emotional state at goal | Progress emotionality | Dopamine modulates the whole system as the common currency for assessing pursuit value and progres…

concepts.title: Good Debt vs Bad Debt
concepts.q: When is borrowing considered strategic versus an anchor that drags financial progress?
concepts.a: Good/strategic debt funds assets that can create wealth (mortgage, business loan used deliberately). Bad debt finances consumption—cars, gadgets, lifestyle—that does not build net worth and often traps people in interest.
concepts.definition: Debt taxonomy — debt is not uniformly evil; consumer debt is the anchor that drags progress. mark tilbury and khan academy / financial literacy tina huang share the framing with different emphasis. Good / strategic debt (when used deliberately): - Mortgage — property may appreciate; leverage borrowed money for long-term net worth - Business loan — Mark's first RC model shop couldn't open without a loan; profit justified the risk Bad debt (avoid): - Consumer debt — financing cars, gadgets, lifestyle items that don't create wealth - UK example: ~2.2 million drivers finance cars; most stuck in a money trap - Rule: if you can't afford it outright and it won't create wealth, don't buy it Pairs wi…

concepts.title: Government Censorship Pressure
concepts.q: What kind of content removal pressure did Telegram face from governments, beyond illegal violent material?
concepts.a: Systematic attempts to coerce removal of peaceful political speech and opposition channels. Documented cases include French intelligence requests and Russian Navalny-group pressure; Durov discloses meetings and refuses intelligence NDAs.
concepts.definition: Systematic government attempts to coerce Telegram into removing political speech — not illegal violent content but peaceful opposition channels. Documented cases: French intelligence requesting Romania/Moldova conservative channel bans while Durov detained; Russian 2011 VK Navalny groups; French police far-left protest channel (refused). Durov discloses all meetings; refuses NDAs with intelligence services.

concepts.title: GP Incentive Metformin Quota
concepts.q: How did UK quality targets for metformin prescribing punish doctors whose type-2 diabetes patients remitted through diet?
concepts.a: A required percentage of T2D patients on metformin counted as good practice. Stopping the drug after diet-driven remission looked like poor practice and cost the GP financially, incentivizing prescription over behavioral reversal.

concepts.title: Grade Homework Not Do Homework
concepts.q: As AI handles more execution, what skill should learners prioritize over producing the first draft themselves?
concepts.a: Learn to grade homework: verify, critique, and extend AI-generated solutions rather than only performing repetitive computation. Humans become quick analytical judges of correctness who generate their own approach.
concepts.definition: Shift in human learning purpose as AI handles execution: students historically learned how to do homework and pass exams; in the AI era everyone must learn how to grade homework — verify, critique, and extend AI-generated solutions rather than perform repetitive computation themselves. po shen loh's software-engineer analogy: given an unfamiliar task, engineers search Stack Overflow for similar examples and technologies — they don't expect copy-paste complete code, but use results to research. With ChatGPT, the first step in any new problem becomes asking AI; the human must be a quick analytical thinker judging correctness and generating their own approach. Pairs directly with knowledge chea…

concepts.title: Graduate Independent Learning Goal
concepts.q: What educational outcome does Po-Shen Loh prioritize over keeping students enrolled in classes indefinitely?
concepts.a: Make students independent learners as fast as possible so they no longer need classes from anyone. Finish foundational curriculum, discover you can learn anything, and reject lifetime-customer-value retention models.
concepts.definition: po shen loh's anti-subscription education philosophy: the goal is not classes for every year of life — it's to make students independent learners as fast as possible so they "don't need any classes from anyone ever again." Finish the curriculum (algebra, geometry, combinatorics, number theory via novel-problem thinking) and discover you can learn anything. Social entrepreneurship alignment: expii explicitly rejects maximizing lifetime customer value when the mission is solving the customer's problem (becoming a self-sufficient thinker) quickly. Contrast ed-tech models optimizing retention over graduation.

concepts.title: Grantham Crash Portfolio
concepts.q: For average wage investors bracing for a US AI-bubble crash, what defensive mix does Grantham favor and what does he exclude?
concepts.a: Roughly ~60% non-US equities, 5–10% precious metals, optional real estate, remainder bonds/cash. Explicit exclusions: no US stocks (especially US tech) and no crypto; diversify and hold for a long horizon.
concepts.definition: jeremy grantham's defensive allocation for average wage/salary investors ahead of ai investment bubble burst: | Slice | Allocation | Notes | |-------|------------|-------| | Non-US equities | ~60% | Broad world ex-US or EM index; cheaper vs US | | Precious metals | 5–10% | Gold + silver | | Real estate | Optional | If convenient/sensible — but housing overvaluation grantham caution | | Bonds + cash | Remainder | US Treasuries via TreasuryDirect.gov; corporate bonds via brokers | Explicit exclusions: no US stocks (especially US tech — sell all if overweight); no crypto (bitcoin zero thesis grantham). Rule #1: diversify. Hold for long horizon; avoid trading; recognize US outperformance cycle m…

concepts.title: Granular Emotion Labeling
concepts.q: Why is saying you feel 'upset' or 'fine' a weak starting point for emotion regulation?
concepts.a: Labeling drives strategy selection. Distinguishing anxiety vs fear vs pressure vs stress (and finer grains) lets you pick the right regulation tool; vague vocabulary blocks effective next steps.
concepts.definition: Labeling drives strategy selection — vague "fine" or "upset" fails. Distinguish anxiety vs fear vs pressure vs stress (and finer grains) to pick the right regulation tool. Most people's emotion vocabulary is "awful" per Brackett. Huberman notes hairsplitting risk but agrees precision matters for strategy. Core step in ruler mood meter framework (Label) and prerequisite for effective regulation.

concepts.title: Gray Hair Reversal Window
concepts.q: Under what condition can stress-related gray hair re-darken, and when is that window closed?
concepts.a: Gray follows a threshold model: if energy availability improves before the hair crosses an energetic damage barrier, pigment can return. Very old gray segments (e.g., ~10 years) are too far below threshold to regain color.
concepts.definition: Gray hair depigmentation follows a threshold model with a window of reversibility — stress-driven graying can re-darken if energy availability improves before the hair crosses an energetic damage barrier; very old gray segments (e.g. 10 years) are too far below threshold to regain color.

concepts.title: Gray Hair Stress Reversal
concepts.q: What does reversible gray hair under reduced stress imply about aging markers?
concepts.a: Depigmentation links to cellular/metabolic stress, not a purely irreversible aging clock. Picard lab evidence shows graying can be at least temporarily reversible when stress decreases—aging markers need not only march forward.
concepts.definition: Hair graying (depigmentation) is linked to cellular/metabolic stress, not a purely linear irreversible aging clock. Picard lab showed graying can be at least temporarily reversible when stress decreases — challenging the assumption that aging markers always march forward.

concepts.title: Great Compression Inequality Reduction
concepts.q: What social and economic features marked the post–WWII decades when US inequality lessened?
concepts.a: The Great Compression featured stronger worker power, social capital, and more balanced material aspirations—fewer work hours, less household debt, more meaning. Policy echoes include unions, antitrust, CEO-pay limits, and fairer market outcomes.
concepts.definition: Historical period (post–Second World War decades) when US inequality lessened — the "Great Compression." juliet schor (via matt davella): imperfect on racism/sexism, but positive on social capital, public-life involvement, democratizing government, and worker power. Outcomes included fewer work hours, less household debt, more balanced material aspirations, and greater meaning/purpose. Policy mix Schor advocates echoing that era: break up monopolies when corporations abuse consumers/workers; build unions and community democratic power; limit CEO pay; fairer market outcomes before redistribution — not purely tax-and-transfer. Contrast: post-1975 divergence documented in wealth inequality rese…

concepts.title: Great Rewiring of Childhood
concepts.q: What stacked technology and culture shift between about 2010 and 2015 redefined childhood according to Haidt?
concepts.a: Childhood flipped from play-based free outdoor life to always-connected pocket internet plus front-facing cameras and engagement-optimized social apps. Learning, identity, relationships, and reward circuitry rewired during a puberty-sensitive period.
concepts.definition: jonathan haidt's label for 2010–2015 — the half-decade when childhood flipped from phone based vs play based childhood (outdoor free play, unsupervised adventure, in-person conflict resolution) to always-connected pocket internet + front-facing cameras + Instagram/Snapchat/TikTok. Not a single device launch but a stacked technology+culture shift: iPhone (2007) → mass smartphone (~2010–2012) → social apps + algorithms optimized for engagement. "Affects everything about what children and teenagers are doing" — learning, identity, sex/relationship scripts, competition norms, and reward circuitry all rewired on for-profit ad platforms during puberty sensitive period identity. Mental-health data …

concepts.title: Grid Excess Power
concepts.q: How could AI data centers use idle grid capacity without forcing constant six-nines uptime and massive grid expansion?
concepts.a: Grids run ~60% of peak most of the time (~40% idle). Contract for graceful degradation at rare peaks—slow compute, shift loads, use backups—and tiered utility guarantees instead of perfection that forces expansion.
concepts.definition: Using the ~40% idle capacity on power grids (running ~60% of peak 99% of the time) for AI data centers — with contractual graceful degradation during rare peak demand (slow compute, shift workloads, backup generators) instead of requiring six-nines uptime that forces grid expansion.

concepts.title: Grief vs Depression Distinction
concepts.q: Why can grief look like depression yet still call for a different framing and toolkit?
concepts.a: Symptoms overlap (sleep, appetite, low mood), but grief is a time-bounded remapping of attachment with motivation/craving circuitry active on imaging. Depression is a separate syndrome; treating grief as generic depression misguides duration and tools.
concepts.definition: Grief and depression can feel similar and share symptom overlap: - Loss of appetite - Sleep disruption - Crying without apparent trigger - Low mood But Huberman emphasizes they are distinct processes with different neural signatures. Brain imaging (fMRI) in grief prominently activates motivation, craving, and pursuit circuits — not only sadness/depression networks. Grief is a time-bounded process (beginning, middle, end) tied to remapping a specific space time closeness attachment map after loss. Complicated/prolonged grief exists when remapping stalls — may need clinical intervention, but is still grief-framed. Depression is a separate psychiatric syndrome — self-diagnosis conflation is com…

concepts.title: Grief Without Performative Suffering
concepts.q: Why is intensity of suffering a poor measure of how much you loved someone who died?
concepts.a: Suffering intensity does not prove love. You can keep functioning and still love the person without performative prolonged pain; continued life is not the same as indifference.
concepts.definition: The view that suffering intensity is not a valid measure of love for someone lost. Separates continued functioning and love from indifference, while still allowing grief.
keywords: Suffering Is Not Proof of Love

concepts.title: Grill Me Skill
concepts.q: What is “Grill Me Skill” and why does it matter?
concepts.a: matt pocock's adversarial-interviewer procedure skill — his replacement for plan mode. Instead of the agent writing a plan for you to review, the agent grills you: it lists the consequential decisions, asks questions one at a time until ~98% clarity, and builds a shared design concept (Brooks, The Design of Design) before any implementation. Original skill is ~4 sentences; variants include grill-with-docs (adds a glossary and architectural decision records to capture the non-obvious stuff) and grill me for knowledge extraction in second-brain contexts. Both rely on a central reference grilling skill that teaches the LLM how to grill. v1.1 reliability fixes: - Why one question at a time — "as…
concepts.definition: matt pocock's adversarial-interviewer procedure skill — his replacement for plan mode. Instead of the agent writing a plan for you to review, the agent grills you: it lists the consequential decisions, asks questions one at a time until ~98% clarity, and builds a shared design concept (Brooks, The Design of Design) before any implementation. Original skill is ~4 sentences; variants include grill-with-docs (adds a glossary and architectural decision records to capture the non-obvious stuff) and grill me for knowledge extraction in second-brain contexts. Both rely on a central reference grilling skill that teaches the LLM how to grill. v1.1 reliability fixes: - Why one question at a time — "as…

concepts.title: Grill-With-Docs Main Flow
concepts.q: What is “Grill-With-Docs Main Flow” and why does it matter?
concepts.a: The canonical "idea to ship" pipeline in matt pocock's skills repo (as of v1.1), walked top-to-bottom — ideally in one unbroken context window when the work is small enough: 1. grill-with-docs (grill me skill variant) — adversarial interview seeded by a vague idea. The skill explores the codebase, asks follow-up questions (typically ~20, as few as 6 for small work) until the human and agent reach shared understanding. It is stateful: learnings are recorded in context.md and ADRs, so grilling compounds across sessions. Run in normal/auto mode, not plan mode. For planning too big for one session, wayfinder skill replaces this step. 2. prototype (optional) — only if an open question needs a run…
concepts.definition: The canonical "idea to ship" pipeline in matt pocock's skills repo (as of v1.1), walked top-to-bottom — ideally in one unbroken context window when the work is small enough: 1. grill-with-docs (grill me skill variant) — adversarial interview seeded by a vague idea. The skill explores the codebase, asks follow-up questions (typically ~20, as few as 6 for small work) until the human and agent reach shared understanding. It is stateful: learnings are recorded in context.md and ADRs, so grilling compounds across sessions. Run in normal/auto mode, not plan mode. For planning too big for one session, wayfinder skill replaces this step. 2. prototype (optional) — only if an open question needs a run…

concepts.title: GRIN Behavior Change Model
concepts.q: In Unwin's GRIN behavior-change model, what four steps replace vague 'lose weight' advice?
concepts.a: Goals (specific preferred health future), Resources (what the patient already brings), Increments (small realistic next steps), and Notice (reflect on benefits when things go well—identity, energy, self-esteem).

concepts.title: Grok Build Claude Code Compatibility
concepts.q: Why does zero-config Claude Code ecosystem support matter for an alternative agent CLI like Grok Build?
concepts.a: Skills, plugins, hooks, and marketplaces work without migration, lowering switching cost. As agent CLIs commoditize, differentiation shifts to model quality and quotas—compatibility alone does not fix those limits.
concepts.definition: grok build ships with zero-configuration compatibility for the Claude Code ecosystem — skills, plugins, hooks, and marketplaces work without migration. Also supports AGENTS.md conventions.

concepts.title: Grok Build Rate Limits
concepts.q: What happened to usable multi-hour vibe-coding sessions under Super Grok Heavy quotas in BridgeMind's test?
concepts.a: About 8% of a two-week quota burned in ~1 hour, with sub-agent launches mostly failing or partial. Normal 6–8 hour sessions became economically impossible despite the expensive subscription.
concepts.definition: Usage quotas on xAI's Super Grok Heavy subscription (~$300/mo at June 2026) that throttle grok build agent sessions. BridgeMind reports 8% of a two-week quota consumed in ~1 hour — with sub-agent launches mostly failing or partial — making normal 6–8 hour vibe-coding sessions economically impossible. Quota resets on ~two-week cycles (creator observed May 31 reset). usage CLI command shows credit consumption.

concepts.title: Ground Living Postures
concepts.q: Why does Kelly Starrett link daily floor-based sitting and squatting to lower fall risk and hip problems in aging?
concepts.a: Bodies normalize ranges they repeatedly use. Ground living restores hip flexion/external rotation that chairs remove, attenuating fall risk and osteoarthritis burden—not paleo romance, but positional exposure.
concepts.definition: Daily floor-based positions — cross-legged squat, long sit, side-99, fidgeting on the ground — that cultures shapes common in ground-living cultures: toilet on the ground, sleep on the ground. kelly starrett links accumulated exposure to attenuated fall risk in elderly populations and lower hip/low-back osteoarthritis burden — not romanticizing paleo, but restoring ranges modern furniture removes. Mechanism-agnostic framing: bodies normalize what they repeatedly touch. Missing hip flexion/external rotation from chairs alone may drive downstream pain and mobility loss.

concepts.title: Ground Sitting Mobility Habit
concepts.q: How can 20–30 minutes of evening floor sitting improve hips and hamstrings without a dedicated stretch session?
concepts.a: Cross-legged, long sit, 90/90, and squat variations while relaxing expose ranges chairs never provide. Fidgeting when uncomfortable is fine; exposure itself is the first-order mobility stimulus.
concepts.definition: Daily 20–30 minute evening habit of sitting on the floor — cross-legged, long sit, 90/90, squat variations — while watching TV or relaxing. Fidget freely when uncomfortable; the behavior itself cultures hip/hamstring/ankle exposure chairs never provide. Starrett: exposure is the first order of magnitude in restoring normative ranges; this alone often improves hamstrings and hips without dedicated stretch sessions. Initial soreness when returning to floor sitting is expected; differs from needing a full warmup to achieve gym ROM.

concepts.title: Growing Pains Alignment Confirmation
concepts.q: How can feeling low or 'not all the way there' be reframed as useful information rather than pure failure?
concepts.a: Big Sean frames that dissonance as a blessing signal you are out of alignment and meant for something different. Satisfaction where you are would not produce that discomfort; recognition starts release and emotional armor.
concepts.definition: big sean's reframe: feeling low, depressed, or "not all the way there" is often a blessing signal — confirmation you are out of alignment and meant for something different or greater. If you were satisfied where you are, that dissonance wouldn't show up. Recognition → first step out: release baggage weighing you down; build emotional armor so setbacks affect you without hijacking mood permanently.

concepts.title: Growth Assets Risk Scale
concepts.q: On Tilbury's growth-asset ladder, where should most people start, and what sits at the high-risk end?
concepts.a: Start with index funds (then skills/real estate as confidence grows). Higher risk includes online businesses, individual stocks, and alternatives like crypto/collectibles—never risk more than you can lose on moonshots.
concepts.definition: mark tilbury's ordered ladder for deploying the 25% growth portion of the 25 15 50 10 rule. Assets rank from relatively safe/steady to high-risk/high-reward: 1. Index funds — buy the whole market (S&P 500); no stock-picking; set and forget. Where most people should start. 2. Real estate — rental property or REITs (fractional building ownership without buying property). 3. Income skills — copywriting, editing, sales, coding; fastest ROI but takes time; no one can take it away. 4. Online businesses — dropshipping, digital products; big payoff potential, high effort, expect failures. 5. Individual stocks — only with homework; otherwise guessing; keep small, treat as hobby. 6. Alternative invest…

concepts.title: Growth Hormone Secretagogues & Risks
concepts.q: What major risks temper enthusiasm for GH secretagogues despite skin/sleep/recovery upsides?
concepts.a: They are not mutagenic but may act as growth factors for existing tumors; they can raise insulin resistance, PSA/BPH concerns, and hypertrophy risks at high IGF-1. MK-677 also drives hunger, prolactin, and cortisol bleed.
concepts.definition: GH secretagogues (GHRH/GHRP) stimulate pituitary growth-hormone release indirectly (vs exogenous GH). Examples: tesamorelin (FDA-approved for HIV lipodystrophy), ipamorelin, sermorelin; MK-677 is oral ghrelin agonist (non-peptide) causing strong non-pulsatile GH bleed. Somatopause: GH/IGF-1 decline ~30s — proponents cite skin, sleep, recovery, thymic regrowth (thymic involution immunity); opponents cite antagonistic pleiotropy (GH-deficient models live longer; Great Dane vs Chihuahua IGF-1 lifespan inverse). Risks: - Not mutagenic but potential tumor growth factor for existing occult cancers — no good body-count epidemiology. - Insulin resistance — A1C rises; bodybuilding joke: "get lean eno…

concepts.title: Growth Hormone Secretagogues
concepts.q: How do growth-hormone secretagogues differ from injecting GH, and what life stage do consumers often target?
concepts.a: They stimulate pituitary GH release rather than supplying exogenous GH. Consumer use often targets somatopause in the ~30s when IGF-1 drops, aiming for skin, sleep, recovery, and appearance effects under clinical monitoring.
concepts.definition: Growth hormone secretagogues (GHS) stimulate pituitary GH release (not exogenous GH). Used clinically for pediatric short stature, HIV lipodystrophy (tesamorelin FDA-approved), post-burn recovery. Consumer use targets somatopause (~30s) — parallel to menopause/andropause where IGF-1 drops sharply.

concepts.title: GSD (Get Shit Done)
concepts.q: How does the GSD Claude Code plugin fight mid-build context rot without trying to save tokens?
concepts.a: It spawns fresh sub-agents per task with clean context while the main session stays lean, plus quality gates like scope-drop detection and security checks. Sub-agents cost tokens but prevent redoing work the model forgot.
concepts.definition: Claude Code plugin installed via npx get-shit-done-cc --claude --global. Context engineering for long builds: spawns fresh sub-agents per task, each with a clean context window, while the main session stays lean. Addresses dumb zone — mid-session forgetting, corner-cutting, and false "done" declarations. Built-in quality gates: - Scope-drop detection — catches planners silently dropping requirements - Security enforcement — verification anchored to your threat model - Autonomous mode — hand off a spec; plan → execute → commit without babysitting Not a token saver — sub-agents cost tokens — but saves hours redoing work Claude forgot. Use /gsd-help for commands.

concepts.title: Guitar Tone Identity
concepts.q: Why can listeners often identify a great guitarist from a single note rather than from speed alone?
concepts.a: Identity lives in vibrato, attack, timbre, phrasing, and decades of gear/setup choices. Tone and space between ideas act like speech; recognizable touch beats raw speed as the signature.
concepts.definition: Great guitarists are recognizable from minimal audio — often a single note's vibrato, attack, or timbre reveals identity (B.B. King vibrato, Brian May tone, Gilmour's melodic space, Van Halen's touch). Beato proposes (and Lex challenges) a "recognize the guitarist from one note" test using separated tracks. Tone emerges from decades of gear choices (Gilmour's preserved Binson Echorec/Hiwatt pedals), phrasing (space between ideas like speech), and deliberate setup — not speed alone.

concepts.title: Habit Automaticity
concepts.q: What two signs show a habit has become truly automatic rather than still effortful?
concepts.a: High context independence (survives travel and schedule changes) and low limbic friction (little conscious override needed)—like brushing teeth even after terrible sleep.
concepts.definition: When neural circuits execute a behavior reflexively with minimal top-down override — the end goal of habit formation. Low limbic friction + high context independence = deep automaticity (e.g. brushing teeth even after terrible sleep).

concepts.title: Habit Change Four Steps (Swart)
concepts.q: What four neuroplasticity-backed steps does Tara Swart use to change patterns like people-pleasing or procrastination?
concepts.a: Raised awareness (spot pattern and underlying beliefs), focused attention (map triggers and consequences), deliberate practice (rehearse new behavior), and accountability (external support when resistance spikes).
concepts.definition: tara swart's neuroplasticity-backed behavior-change protocol for patterns that don't serve you (overthinking, people-pleasing, repeated relationship mistakes, procrastination): 1. Raised awareness (~50% of the battle) — spot the pattern; dig below conscious thoughts to underlying beliefs (e.g., dating unavailable partners → self-worth wound) 2. Focused attention — map triggers across contexts; marry each decision to its consequence ("you are the sum of every decision") 3. Deliberate practice — seek scenarios to practice the new behavior; old childhood pathways resist (energy-hungry brain prefers least resistance); incremental wins (50% → 75% → default) 4. Accountability — external support wh…
keywords: Raised awareness focused attention deliberate practice

concepts.title: Hackable Luck Framework
concepts.q: According to Simon Squibb, how do you 'hack luck' without waiting passively for chance?
concepts.a: Persist with purpose until you outlast competitors, define your own success destination, and put yourself in luck's path by showing up where opportunity lives. Accepting luck means preparation plus presence, not idle waiting.

concepts.title: Haidt Four Phone Norms
concepts.q: What four coordinated norms does Haidt propose to escape the 'everyone needs a phone because peers have one' trap?
concepts.a: No smartphone before high school; no social media until 16; phone-free schools; and more independence/free play/real-world responsibility. Simultaneous adoption across families and schools removes the loneliness penalty of going alone.
concepts.definition: jonathan haidt's four coordinated norms to break the collective action trap where every child "needs" a smartphone because peers have one. Designed to delay exposure past puberty sensitive period identity and restore phone based vs play based childhood adventure. 1. No smartphone before high school — flip phone or simple watch OK; no pocket internet; shared family computer in kitchen fine; no "Billy's iPad in his room." 2. No social media until 16 — no safe minor version when prestige = likes/followers; exposes brand-management performance, shock content ("running the gauntlet"), eating-disorder/Tourette-copying rabbit holes; laws should raise minimum age to 16–18. 3. Phone-free schools — lo…

concepts.title: Hair as Biological History Biomarker
concepts.q: How can a single hair strand serve as a timeline of stress and metabolism?
concepts.a: Hair grows ~1 cm/month and preserves molecular composition from when each segment formed. Stress timelines can correlate with depigmentation bands; mitochondrial proteins rise in white vs dark segments on the same person.
concepts.definition: Human hair encodes biological history along its length — a temporal record of metabolism, stress, and exposures. Same-genome hairs on one head can differ in color and proteomics because they formed during different life periods.

concepts.title: Happiness as Present Contentment
concepts.q: Why can fixing outer problems still leave someone unhappy if they chase parties and peak joy as the definition of happiness?
concepts.a: Mel Robbins reframes the goal as contentment wherever you are—allowing feelings without living 15 steps ahead. Outer fixes do not automatically deliver present peace; nervous-system anchoring matters more than endless hustle.
concepts.definition: mel robbins's redefinition after realizing "happiness" as parties/laughter/full-joy was the wrong target. Real goal: learn to be happy and content wherever you are — not 15 steps ahead, not fixing the emotion, allowing it to rise, then noticing another way to feel. Breakthrough: Vermont deck moment — "exactly where I'm supposed to be" without anxiety, stress, or mile-long to-do energy (Eckhart Tolle Power of Now bench parallel). Critical nuance: fixing outer problems (marriage, money, business) does not automatically deliver this. When nothing outside is rationally wrong, the campaign of misery can turn harder on the self. Practice stack: breath, positive reframe, body/nervous-system anchori…
keywords: Happiness as Contentment Wherever You Are

concepts.title: Harness Engineering
concepts.q: What problem does multi-session agent orchestration with handoff docs solve better than one marathon coding session?
concepts.a: It avoids the dumb-zone of long contexts by chaining planner, implementer, and validator sessions with structured handoffs. The harness makes autonomy more deterministic by choosing when LLM vs plain code runs.
concepts.definition: Building workflows that orchestrate multiple coding agent sessions for larger tasks, with structured handoff documents between phases — instead of one marathon session that hits the dumb zone. Harness (two meanings): 1. Base harness — the agent product itself (Claude Code = system prompt + tools + runtime) 2. Custom harness / AI layer — your CLAUDE.md, skills, hooks, MCP/CLI integrations, and verification systems built on top Goal: make autonomous systems as deterministic as possible — pick when the LLM runs vs when plain code runs (arkon pattern). Assembly-line analogy: planner session → implementer session → validator/reviewer session, each with a handoff doc (plan, execution report).

concepts.title: Haunted Self-Improvement
concepts.q: In Goggins's framing, why must transformation keep you 'haunted' by who you used to be?
concepts.a: The ghost of the old self (abuse, obesity, lies for approval) keeps discipline from switching off. Improvement is not permanent—without daily fighting that former identity, you slide back.
concepts.definition: Core Goggins concept from how to build immense inner strength david goggins: transformation requires becoming haunted by who you were — abuse, obesity, illiteracy, lies for approval — so discipline never switches off. Huberman highlights "haunted" as the episode's anchor word.

concepts.title: Head of AI Role
concepts.q: How does a modern Head of AI differ from a purely advisory AI strategy role?
concepts.a: They own strategy and stay hands-on: map processes, build with tools, ship to production, prioritize use cases, hire, and manage change. The role is spreading beyond large enterprises as CEOs appoint AI-officer equivalents.
concepts.definition: Emerging executive function owning AI strategy and implementation across an organization (or group of companies). Not purely advisory — modern Head of AI stays hands-on (map processes, build with tools like claude code, ship to production) while also prioritizing use cases, hiring, and change management. IBM survey: 76% of 2,000 CEOs report a chief-AI-officer equivalent (up from 26% ~24 months earlier); role is spreading beyond large enterprises.

concepts.title: Healing Expectation & Clock Time
concepts.q: How can a doctor's stated recovery timeline shape how quickly a wound or fracture seems to heal?
concepts.a: Ellen Langer argues stated timelines organize physiology—patients organize body and life around population averages. Framing recovery as unknown with room for faster healing preserves possibility; treat timelines as hypotheses, not laws.
concepts.definition: ellen langer's argument that stated recovery timelines organize physiology — wounds and fractures heal partly on clock time of expectation, not only tissue biology. Mechanism discussed: Ask a doctor "how long until this broken arm heals?" — they give a conventional answer ("a month") based on population averages, not your specific case. Patient organizes life and body around that frame. Alternative framing — "we don't know; some heal in two weeks; faster is fine" — preserves room for faster recovery. Langer's wound-healing research (referenced): healing tracked real clock time in ways that surprised researchers who assumed perception of time wouldn't matter. Practical implication: Treat medi…

concepts.title: Healthspan Sick-Years Gap
concepts.q: What happens when lifespan keeps rising while healthy life expectancy falls?
concepts.a: People spend many late-life years—up to ~23 in UK figures, nearly a quarter of life—managing chronic illness. The US shows an especially large healthspan–lifespan gap despite high healthcare spend.

concepts.title: Healthy Boundaries in Relationships
concepts.q: What makes a boundary healthy rather than a rigid wall or people-pleasing absence of limits?
concepts.a: Limits set through self-inquiry about needs and the other's intentions. Boundaries protect the 'me' pole so genuine mutual connection can exist; without them, generative giving becomes exploitation.
concepts.definition: Healthy boundaries are limits set through self-inquiry — not rigid walls or people-pleasing absence of limits. paul conti uses the friend who walks into your house unannounced: options include locking the door, accepting the pattern, or recognizing envy/entitlement ("I'm going to walk into your house and make you feel anxious") and exiting a non-friendship. Boundaries protect the me pole so a genuine magic bridge of us can exist.

concepts.title: Healthy Narcissism Shadow Integration
concepts.q: How can narcissism be channeled productively instead of denied as something only other people have?
concepts.a: Narcissism is neither purely good nor bad; healthy path turns self-love outward into empathy. Integrating shadow traits (envy, aggression, grandiosity) lets you acknowledge and deploy dark emotions rather than moral superiority blocking change.
concepts.definition: From Greene's Laws of Human Nature discussion on DOAC: narcissism is neither objectively good nor bad — it's a human consequence. Early childhood (around 3–4) when parental attention shifts builds self-esteem anchor / self-love shreds — necessary unless abuse blocks it. Deep narcissists lack inner anchor → rage, drama for attention. Healthy path: take self-love and turn it outward into empathy; become a healthy narcissist (Greene's term). Shadow integration: Everyone has irrationality, envy, aggression, grandiosity — Gandhi and MLK were strategic; MLK had flawed private life. Moral superiority ("I'm not a narcissist") blocks awareness and change. Greene wrote Laws of Human Nature confronting…

concepts.title: Heat Shock Proteins (HSPs)
concepts.q: What do heat shock proteins do during short sauna-like heat exposure, and when does activation become a problem?
concepts.a: They rescue proteins that would otherwise misfold under temperature stress. Short-term activation from deliberate heat (e.g., 80–100°C, 5–20 min) is beneficial; prolonged chronic activation becomes problematic.
concepts.definition: Heat shock proteins are protective molecules deployed during temperature stress to rescue proteins that would otherwise misfold — analogous to how cooking permanently changes meat protein structure, but in living cells the goal is preventing detrimental misfolding during transient heat. Short-term HSP activation is beneficial; prolonged chronic activation becomes problematic. Multiple HSP varieties travel through brain and body preventing wrong-fold configurations in heated cells. Deliberate heat exposure in the deliberate heat exposure protocols range (80–100°C, 5–20 min) activates HSPs in both animal models and humans — one mechanism linking sauna to reduced mortality and possible lifespan…

concepts.title: Henneman Size Principle
concepts.q: Do you always need very heavy weights to recruit high-threshold motor units for hypertrophy?
concepts.a: No. Motor units recruit low-to-high threshold as load or sustained effort rises. Research supports ~30–80% of 1RM as sufficient when volume and effort are adequate—heavy helps but is not required for most people.
concepts.definition: Foundational muscle-physiology rule (andrew huberman): motor units (nerve-to-muscle connections) recruit in order from low threshold → high threshold — a conservation-of-energy staircase. - Light load → minimum motor units engaged - Heavy load → more units immediately - Sustained effort on moderate loads also recruits higher-threshold units over time High-threshold recruitment opens the gate for strength and hypertrophy adaptations. Common online misread: "you must lift heavy to hit high-threshold units." Research supports 30–80% of 1RM (moderately light to heavy) as sufficient when volume and effort are adequate — heavy weights help but are not required for most people offsetting age-relate…

concepts.title: Here-and-Now Molecules
concepts.q: How do serotonin, endocannabinoids, oxytocin, and prolactin balance dopamine's 'want more' drive?
concepts.a: They bias contentment with what you already have and interoceptive present-moment focus. Healthy motivation needs both pursuit (dopamine/exteroception) and here-and-now contentment systems.
concepts.definition: Neuromodulators that bias the brain toward contentment with what you already have — the complement to dopamine's pursuit-of-more system. Described in The Molecule of More (Lieberman & Long) as the "here and now" side of motivation. Key players: serotonin (bliss/contentment from raphe nuclei), endocannabinoids (present-moment bliss, cannabis binds these receptors), oxytocin, and prolactin (satiety after reward). Together they enable interoception — focus on sensations inside your skin — vs dopamine's exteroceptive drive toward external goals.
keywords: Molecule of More here and now

concepts.title: Heteropessimism
concepts.q: What cultural narrative claims heterosexual men and women cannot form stable partnerships, and what does relationship data push back with?
concepts.a: Heteropessimism (~2019) amplifies internet gender-war dynamics and app asymmetries. Close-relationship data show men and women often want similar outcomes; acquaintance and time shrink perceived gaps that stranger/app contexts maximize.
concepts.definition: The cultural narrative (coined ~2019) that heterosexual men and women cannot get along or form stable partnerships — amplified by internet "junior high" dynamics, clickbait, and asymmetric swiping behavior on apps.

concepts.title: High-Performance Decentralization Holy Grail
concepts.q: What 'impossible' blockchain target tries to match real-world finance throughput without sacrificing permissionless decentralization?
concepts.a: Rails fast enough for infrastructure-scale TPS while remaining sufficiently decentralized—solving the trilemma without Solana-like validator cost barriers or Ethereum-like throughput ceilings.
concepts.definition: The "impossible" target: blockchain rails fast enough for real-world financial infrastructure (NYSE ~2M TPS cited) while remaining sufficiently decentralized for permissionless participation — solving the blockchain trilemma without accepting Solana-style validator cost barriers or Ethereum-style throughput limits.

concepts.title: High-trust remote work arbitrage
concepts.q: In Po-Shen Loh's AI-era hiring prediction, why do trust networks plus wage differences matter more than anonymous job boards?
concepts.a: Remote work works, but hiring follows people you already know. Splitting cross-border wage differentials can save employers money while remote workers live well—if capability was proven through real relationships.
concepts.definition: po shen loh's hiring prediction for the AI era: remote work functions well, but who you hire follows trust networks — founders call people they already know from cross-border coaching friendships, not anonymous job boards. Combined with wage arbitrage (what $1 buys differs vastly by country), splitting the wage differential lets the employer save massively while the remote worker lives "really, really well." Prerequisite: detecting genuinely capable people through direct relationships (paired teaching, thoughtful-network membership) rather than exam rankings. Safety to start companies often requires first securing a trusted remote income, then leveraging the same network for startup resource…

concepts.title: Highly Palatable Food Reset
concepts.q: Why can broccoli taste bland after ultra-savory processed foods, and how long can a reset take?
concepts.a: Highly palatable foods raise dopamine comparison baselines so whole foods taste dull. About 48 hours without them can restore sensitivity so simple seasoned foods taste good again.
concepts.definition: Highly palatable (ultra-savory/sweet processed) foods shift dopamine such that whole foods taste bland by comparison — same peak-baseline logic as other indulgences. ~48 hours without highly palatable foods restores sensitivity; simple whole foods (e.g., seasoned broccoli) taste good again. Mechanism: prior dopamine peaks from richer foods set an elevated comparison baseline.

concepts.title: Hip Shoulder Breath Spin Up
concepts.q: What 8–10 minute morning homework does Starrett recommend to restore shapes missing from desk life?
concepts.a: Pick one spin-up: hip (back/knee pain), shoulder (shoulder/neck pain, often rope flow), or breath otherwise. Morning exposure makes later movement easier and diagnoses mobility deficits.
concepts.definition: the ready state 8–10 minute morning homework: choose one of three spin-up protocols — hip, shoulder, or breath — to touch crucial shapes absent from desk life. Routing: back/knee pain → hip spin-up; shoulder/neck pain → shoulder spin-up (often includes David Weck rope flow); otherwise cycle through. Purpose: morning exposure so later-day movement is easier; diagnostic — suffering during hip spin-up signals movement history deficits. Athletes: after bathroom/hydration at the gym, add play based warmup protocol (5–10 min) before main work.

concepts.title: HIT Low-Volume Training (Yates)
concepts.q: What defines Yates-style HIT volume and intensity versus typical high-volume bodybuilding?
concepts.a: Few sessions per week (~45–60 min), a couple warm-ups then 1–2 working sets to failure (optional drop set). Intensity and recovery matter more than adding sessions; natural lifters should not copy steroid-supported volume.
concepts.definition: dorian yates's Blood and Guts protocol — hybrid between Arthur Jones / mike mentzer pure HIT and conventional high-volume bodybuilding: - Frequency: 2–3 whole-body or split sessions/week (~45–60 min); natural lifters often 2×/week sufficient - Sets: Couple warm-ups → 1–2 working sets to muscular failure training; optional drop set (−10% load, fail again) - Intensity over volume: More stimulus failed Yates when he added a 4th weekly session — 3–4 week plateau, regressed when cut back - Recovery-first: Enough to stimulate, not more than recoverable (stimulate recover adapt training); steroid users recover faster — don't copy pro volume drug-free - Cardio separate: Weights build/maintain muscle…

concepts.title: Holding Space
concepts.q: What listening practice makes others feel seen and understood better than monopolizing conversation with advice?
concepts.a: Holding space: give airtime without interrupting, judging, or rushing to advice—aim near 50/50 speaking. Use curiosity questions, pauses, and emotional reflection instead of hijacking with your own story.
concepts.definition: Vinh Giang's fifth and "most unfair" communication secret: making others feel seen, heard, understood, and connected by giving them airtime instead of monopolizing conversation. Ideal baseline is 50/50 speaking; most people talk the entire time. Holding space means listening without interrupting, judging, or rushing to advice — creating psychological safety, often without saying a word. Professional payoff: people share real worries, desires, and motivations you can address — insight competitors miss.

concepts.title: Holy trinity (business offer)
concepts.q: What three elements must every simple business offer combine, per Ali Abdaal?
concepts.a: Person (who you help), problem/outcome (what they want but cannot get alone), and solution (your product or service bridging the gap). Expertise you already sell to an employer can become a freelance offer with more clients.

concepts.title: Homelessness Addiction Treatment Policy
concepts.q: Why is street homelessness rarely solved by individual clinical medicine alone in low-unemployment eras?
concepts.a: Modern street homelessness skews heavily toward mental illness plus addiction. Effective responses combine sober housing (e.g., Oxford House), recovery culture, and sometimes leveraged care like drug courts—with civil-rights protections.
concepts.definition: Modern street homelessness skews mental illness + addiction (low unemployment era). ~50%+ substance component (Huberman estimate; Humphreys agrees pattern). Oxford House model: resident-run sober housing (~10K houses); rent contribution; no violence/substances — strong evidence some leave streets voluntarily. Leveraged treatment: civil commitment if gravely disabled/imminent harm (like returning wandering Alzheimer's patient); drug courts — treatment instead of jail for crimes (phone theft etc.). Addiction impairs judgment like dementia — pressure often required (91% got lean-on before treatment). Cannot solve via individual clinical medicine alone — needs housing + recovery culture + someti…

concepts.title: Homo technae tool relationship
concepts.q: Why does Catie Cuan prefer defining humans by their relationship to tools rather than by intelligence alone?
concepts.a: Many species are intelligent; Aristotle's Homo technae framing centers tool-making relationships—from stone tools to robots—as definitional to being human, including learning to dance/partner with machines.
concepts.definition: Aristotle's reframing cited by catie cuan: humans should not be called Homo sapiens (many species are intelligent) but Homo technae — defined by our relationship to tools and ability to make them. Dancing with a robot (2017, Amy's lab) felt like standing on a continuum from stone tools, fire, wheel, printing press, computers, to modern robots — "part of definitionally what it means to be a human is to be in relationship with tools."

concepts.title: Hormone Optimization Supplement Stack
concepts.q: What is the point of Kyle Gillett's non-suppressive supplement stack before considering exogenous testosterone?
concepts.a: Improve subjective outcomes—energy, libido, recovery—without chasing arbitrary total-T numbers. Tools like creatine, carnitine, vitamin D, boron, tongkat, and fadogia support endogenous hormone pathways under monitoring.
concepts.definition: Non-suppressive tools kyle gillett recommends before exogenous testosterone — for subjective outcomes (energy, libido, recovery) not arbitrary total-T number chasing. | Supplement | Mechanism / dose | Notes | |---|---|---| | Creatine | ↑ total T; shifts T→DHT conversion; mitochondrial ATP backup | Does not accelerate hair loss beyond baseline maleness; poor reason to skip. See creatine monohydrate, creatine myths debunked | | Beta-alanine | Amino acid synthesis; methionine/homocysteine processing | 1–3 g if creatine non-responder or homocysteine elevated (blood test) | | L-carnitine (oral) | Mitochondrial fatty-acid shuttle; l carnitine androgen receptor density | 1–5 g/day; ~10% oral bioava…

concepts.title: Hormozi Value Equation
concepts.q: In Hormozi's value equation, which factors raise perceived value and which lower it?
concepts.a: Value rises with dream outcome and perceived likelihood of achievement; it falls with time delay plus effort and sacrifice. Speeding the outcome is often the most underused lever in offer design.
concepts.definition: Perceived value rises with dream outcome and perceived likelihood of achievement, and falls with time delay plus effort and sacrifice required. Explains offer design, pricing, and even content consumption.

concepts.title: Hotel Maid Exercise Mindset Study
concepts.q: What changed for hotel maids told their daily work counted as exercise, despite no change in diet or work intensity?
concepts.a: The mindset group lost weight and improved waist-to-hip ratio, BMI, and blood pressure. Langer frames it as nocebo: the activity was already present but psychologically invisible, suppressing physiological benefit.
concepts.definition: ellen langer study with student Alia Crum: hotel chambermaids were randomly split — one group was taught that their daily work counts as exercise (making a bed = gym machine, window cleaning = workout); control group received no reframing. No change in diet or work intensity between groups. Results: The mindset-shift group lost weight, improved waist-to-hip ratio and BMI, and lowered blood pressure — despite already performing the same physical activity before the intervention. Langer frames this as a placebo nocebo mind body demonstration: the "active ingredient" (exercise) was already present but psychologically invisible — lack of recognition suppressed physiological benefit.

concepts.title: House Hacking (FHA Multifamily)
concepts.q: How can owner-occupying a small multifamily property slash housing cost for a first-time buyer?
concepts.a: Live in one unit, rent the others, and use low-down-payment FHA financing (e.g., 3.5%). Rents can cover the mortgage so housing cost approaches zero or turns cash-flow positive while you occupy on-site.
concepts.definition: Owner-occupy a multifamily property (2–4 units), rent other units, live rent-free or cash-flow positive. gabe bult used FHA loans at 3.5% down as first-time buyer living on-site (US); did this twice.

concepts.title: Housing Overvaluation (Grantham)
concepts.q: Why does Grantham argue even a large home-price drop may not restore 'good old days' affordability?
concepts.a: Global residential multiples rose sharply (UK ~3.4× income in 1994 to 10×+). A −30% drop can still leave ~6–7× income—about twice historic norms—while declining family formation reduces future bidders.
concepts.definition: jeremy grantham: residential property historically expensive globally — engineered upward bias since ~1994. UK example: 3.4× family income (1994) → 10×+ today; even −30% drop leaves ~6–7× (still ~2× "good old days"). Same pattern in China, Canada, Australia, most of Europe. Consequences: young couples priced out; rents squeeze; declining family formation + replacement rate commons baby bust → fewer future bidders (can't assume scarcity = forever appreciation). Population decline in rich countries amplifies. Property OK in diversified portfolio but not a slam-dunk wealth builder at current multiples.

concepts.title: How to Talk to Someone With Addiction
concepts.q: What conversational approach works better than lecturing someone into quitting a substance?
concepts.a: Ask what they want from change; treat addiction as pain and shame, not mere character defect. External pressure is common (91% had someone lean on them before alcohol treatment); attending an open AA meeting with them can lower barriers.
concepts.definition: Don't lecture — ask what they want from change. Acknowledge addiction as pain and shame, not mere character defect. External pressure is normal: Doug Pollson study — 91% of people seeking alcohol treatment had someone "lean on them" in past year (spouse ultimatum, boss, lawyer/judge). Family tool: attend open AA meeting yourself ("I went; I'll go with you") lowers barrier. Desire to quit required to attend (can't show up intoxicated).

concepts.title: Huberman Sleep Stack
concepts.q: Which three compounds does Huberman often cite for sleep transition, and which precursor class does he avoid at night?
concepts.a: Magnesium threonate/bisglycinate, apigenin, and theanine before bed to quiet forebrain rumination via GABA-related paths. He avoids direct serotonin/dopamine precursors like 5-HTP/tryptophan at night due to mid-sleep disruption risk.
concepts.definition: Three compounds Huberman cites for sleep transition (consult physician before use): | Compound | Dose / timing | Mechanism | |----------|---------------|-----------| | Magnesium threonate or bisglycinate | 300–400 mg, 30–60 min before sleep | Crosses blood-brain barrier; increases GABA; quiets forebrain rumination | | Apigenin (chamomile compound) | 50 mg, ~30 min before sleep | GABA + chloride channels; reduces evening cortisol and rumination | | Theanine | Per label, combined with above | GABA + chloride channel activation | Combined use shows synergistic "sleep cocktail" effect. Huberman avoids direct serotonin/dopamine precursors (5-HTP, tryptophan) at night — can cause deep sleep then 2…

concepts.title: Huberman Stress Supplements
concepts.q: When behavioral stress tools are not enough, which supplements does Huberman discuss—and which hormone pill does he advise avoiding?
concepts.a: L-theanine for anxiety/sleep transition; ashwagandha only for acute high-stress periods (not year-round). Avoid supplemental melatonin: typical doses are supraphysiological and may affect hormones; behavior remains primary.
concepts.definition: When behavioral tools cannot match life stress load, Huberman cites three non-prescription compounds — with one explicit avoid: | Compound | Use | Dose / notes | |----------|-----|--------------| | L-theanine | Chronic anxiety/stress; sleep transition | 100–200 mg, 30–60 min before sleep if safe; increases GABA; quiets forebrain rumination; 8 studies show relaxation/anxiety reduction | | Ashwagandha | Acute high-stress periods only | Lowers anxiety and cortisol; Huberman does not take year-round — only when short/medium-term stress exceeds management capacity | | Melatonin | Avoid supplementing | Pineal hormone tied to darkness; helps fall asleep not stay asleep; typical 1–3 mg doses are sup…

concepts.title: Human connection economic asset
concepts.q: When AI disseminates information better than people, what human value may still remain the base currency of exchange?
concepts.a: Lived experience and emotional resonance—relating to a patient, live performance, embodied competition, genuine parental worry. Informational work may defer to AI, but connection and presence stay scarce if economies still function.

concepts.title: Human-in-the-Loop vs AFK Tasks
concepts.q: In Matt Pocock's AI workflow taxonomy, which work must stay human-in-the-loop versus what can run AFK overnight?
concepts.a: Alignment, PRD shaping, taste QA, and vertical-slice review stay human. Implementation Ralph loops, parallel runs, and automated checks can be away-from-keyboard after planning—you cannot Ralph-loop the grilling phase.
concepts.definition: Matt Pocock's task taxonomy for the AI age: | Type | Examples | Why | |------|----------|-----| | Human-in-the-loop | Grill Me alignment, PRD shaping, vertical-slice issue review, manual QA for taste | Requires judgment, taste, multi-human mob programming | | AFK (away from keyboard) | Issue implementation Ralph loop, parallel Sand Castle runs, automated typecheck/test review in fresh context | Can queue after planning; "night shift" | Key rule: you cannot Ralph-loop the grilling phase — alignment must stay human-in-the-loop. Implementation is where humans step back. Workflow metaphor: day shift (human plans, reviews slice structure) → night shift (agents drain Kanban).

concepts.title: Human interaction robotics grand challenge
concepts.q: As robots leave narrow factories for homes and hospitals, what unsolved layer still outpaces capability demos?
concepts.a: How humans and robots interact safely, legibly, and meaningfully. Intelligence scales faster than mutual understandability; weekly capability announcements miss this everyday-interaction challenge.
concepts.definition: The persistent unsolved problem in robotics: as machines gain intelligence and leave narrow vertical applications for everyday human spaces, how do humans and robots interact safely, legibly, and meaningfully? Per catie cuan, weekly "ChatGPT moment" capability announcements miss this layer — intelligence scales faster than human robot legibility.

concepts.title: Human-robot legibility
concepts.q: Beyond completing a task, what must robots convey in shared physical spaces with people?
concepts.a: Legibility: predictable safe behavior, clarity about what the machine is doing and why, and emotional comfort. Opaque beeping hospital machines show failure even when functionally useful.
concepts.definition: Making robots understandable to people and people understandable to robots in shared physical spaces. Legibility spans safety (predictable behavior), clarity (what the machine is doing and why), and emotional comfort — not just task completion. Opposite of hospital machines that beep without explanation while patients feel oppressed and confused.

concepts.title: Humanize High-Status People
concepts.q: How do you stay composed around CEOs or celebrities instead of shrinking into fanboy nervousness?
concepts.a: Humanize them mentally—they still have ordinary flaws—and put lessons on the pedestal, not the person. Then connect via story rather than feature lists so rapport replaces idolization.
concepts.definition: Vinh Giang's psychological reframe for staying composed around CEOs, celebrities, and high-status contacts — preventing the fanboy shrink that makes even skilled communicators sound like "a little child."

concepts.title: Humanoid robots blue collar disruption
concepts.q: Why might 'learn a trade; AI only hits white-collar' be a shorter-lived safety assumption?
concepts.a: Humanoid robots are scaling toward factory labor (e.g., Hyundai/Boston Dynamics intent). Once humanoid manufacturing workers proliferate, blue-collar roles face disruption too—not only desk jobs.
concepts.definition: po shen loh's inversion of the "plumber survives AI" assumption: humanoid robots are proliferating — boston dynamics acquired by Hyundai signals intent to deploy robot workers in manufacturing plants ("not for dancing"). Hyundai has capital to scale humanoid factory labor, which will "wreak havoc across the blue collar as well." Challenges blue collar vs white collar ai (Mo Gawdat: white-collar first, blue-collar later) by arguing blue-collar safety is shorter-lived than assumed once humanoid manufacturing scales. Human survival traits shift to trustworthy human ai survival — care, trust, flexibility — not manual dexterity alone.

concepts.title: Humanoid uncanny expectations
concepts.q: What happens to user expectations when a robot looks more human-like in form?
concepts.a: Closer humanoid appearance raises unconscious expectations of human-level capability (mirror neurons). Form can make even capable machines fail socially if performance lags those expectations.
concepts.definition: rodney brooks principle cited by catie cuan: the closer a robot looks to a human (bilateral symmetry, human proportions), the higher expectations people unconsciously assign — mirror neurons activate and users expect human-level capability. Humanoid form in human spaces (homes, hospitals) may fail even capable machines because expectations exceed performance. Industry has locked into a myopic humanoid vision (dishwasher/laundry humanoids) without asking whether users — e.g., a 77-year-old ESL parent — actually want that form factor.

concepts.title: HVAC Noise Cognitive Fatigue
concepts.q: How does constant loud HVAC hum affect mental performance even when you think you are ignoring it?
concepts.a: Ongoing auditory input keeps brainstem vigilance circuits active, increasing mental fatigue and decreasing cognition. Continuous high-level hum is worse than intermittent thermostat cycling; relief when it stops is circuit shutoff.
concepts.definition: Constant, loud HVAC noise (incessant AC or heater hum) significantly increases mental fatigue and decreases cognitive performance (Banbury & Berry 2005; Love/Francis psychophysiology data). Parallel to visual alertness: ongoing auditory input keeps brainstem vigilance circuits (locus coeruleus) active even when ignored — relief when it stops is circuit shutoff. Long white-noise exposure (especially loud, especially in children) may disrupt auditory frequency maps and language processing — intermittent thermostat cycling less concerning than continuous high-level hum. Short bouts of white/pink/brown noise (~45 min) can boost alertness when focus is waning, but not equivalent to 40 hz binaural…

concepts.title: Hybrid E2E Encryption Tradeoff
concepts.q: Why does Telegram keep default cloud chats alongside opt-in Secret Chats instead of full E2E everywhere?
concepts.a: Pure E2E everywhere breaks large groups, persistent history, bots, and multi-device sync. Default encrypted cloud preserves those features; Secret Chats add full E2E for users who accept device-bound limits.
concepts.definition: Telegram offers tiered encryption: default encrypted cloud (sync, large groups, channels, bots, multi-device) vs opt-in Secret Chats (full E2E, no screenshots, no forwarding, device-bound). Pure E2E everywhere breaks 200K-member groups, persistent histories, bot workflows, and team collaboration — Telegram builds Telegram on regular chats, not Secret Chats.

concepts.title: Hydration & Lymphatic Flow
concepts.q: Why can cutting water to reduce 'retention' backfire for lymphatic swelling?
concepts.a: Much interstitial fluid buildup reflects insufficient lymphatic flow rate, not excess drinking. Adequate hydration plus sodium balance supports blood volume so lymph keeps moving.
concepts.definition: Adequate hydration supports lymphatic flow rate — counterintuitive for those trying to reduce "water retention." Much interstitial fluid accumulation reflects insufficient lymphatic flow rate, not excess drinking. Proper blood volume + sodium balance keeps lymph moving.

concepts.title: AI hype dichotomy
concepts.q: How does Mo Gawdat split public AI perception from what labs are actually doing?
concepts.a: Public side is overhyped yet underwhelming (gimmicky demos, escape stories). Lab side is underhyped and alarming: self-improving systems running rapid experiments. Policy lags because people optimize for chatbots, not backend loops.

concepts.title: Hyperactive Hive Mind Workflow
concepts.q: What collaboration pattern does Cal Newport call the hyperactive hive mind, and why is 'check email less' hard alone?
concepts.a: Unstructured ongoing email/Slack/Zoom back-and-forth as the primary coordination mechanism. Constant context switching becomes organizationally required, so individual inbox discipline alone cannot fix it.
concepts.definition: Cal Newport's label for the default modern knowledge-work collaboration pattern: unstructured, ongoing email/Slack/Zoom back-and-forth as the primary coordination mechanism. Creates constant context switching, makes "check email less often" nearly impossible individually because the workflow is systemic — built into how the organization runs. Breaking free requires expensive top-down workflow redesign (new rules, tools, meeting norms) — not willpower alone. Individual pull based workflow helps only where you control the rules.

concepts.title: Hypertrophy Rep Ranges and Failure
concepts.q: According to Galpin, what load and rep bands grow muscle if sets are taken near failure?
concepts.a: About 30–80% of 1RM and roughly 5–30 reps per set produce similar hypertrophy when execution and proximity to failure are solid. Heavy loads help but are not uniquely required.
concepts.definition: andy galpin on muscle growth programming: - Load range: 30–80% of 1RM — evidence-backed band for hypertrophy and strength (henneman size principle); heavy not required for most people - Rep range: 5–30 reps per set — literature shows roughly equal hypertrophy if execution is correct - Must train near muscular failure — not partner-lifts-the-bar extreme, but close; ~10% of total sets/workouts to failure, rest sub-max (eighty twenty exercise rule) - Volume is the primary driver (vs intensity for strength); ≥5 sets/muscle/week to maintain, 5–15 to improve (advanced up to 25–30) - Frequency: recoverable schedule; more damage = longer between sessions for same muscle Three likely drivers: mechani…

concepts.title: Hyponatremia & Water Toxicity
concepts.q: How can drinking huge volumes of plain water during endurance events become dangerous?
concepts.a: Rapid intake without electrolytes dilutes blood sodium (hyponatremia). Kidneys cannot keep up, causing potentially life-threatening water toxicity—especially with heavy sweating.
concepts.definition: Hyponatremia — dangerously low blood sodium, often from drinking large volumes of water in a short time, especially during heavy sweating/endurance events without adequate electrolyte replacement. Mechanism: rapid water intake → sodium dilution + kidney regulation disruption → neurons cannot fire action potentials properly → disorientation, severe mental/physical impairment; can be fatal. Endurance examples: athletes entering stadium disoriented, unable to find finish line after prolonged sweat + over-water + under-sodium. Prevention: balance fluid with sodium and other electrolytes during/after heavy sweat; use galpin equation hydration as baseline, scale up in heat.

concepts.title: Icing Healing Suppression Caution
concepts.q: Why does Kelly Starrett caution against routine ice for acute soft-tissue incidents?
concepts.a: Ice can suppress prostaglandin signals that recruit circulating stem cells to damaged tissue, so cells may 'swing past' the injury. Cooling for pain/swelling may trade off optimal healing chemistry.
concepts.definition: kelly starrett cautions that cold water/ice for acute icing may not optimize healing: ice can suppress prostaglandin release, potentially reducing chemical signals that recruit circulating stem cells to damaged tissue — cells "swing on past" the injury site. Framework: question default RICE reflex; consider what upregulates maximal human healing rate. Cross-link cold heat exercise timing — Galpin/Huberman separate post-hypertrophy cold (blunts adaptation) from this acute injury icing debate. Also references ibuprofen usage study (Australian military tactical athletes) as parallel anti-inflammatory concern. Not a blanket ban on cold — nuance between recovery modality, performance timing, and …

concepts.title: Identity Beyond Success Failure
concepts.q: How should you hold successes and failures so setbacks do not collapse who you are?
concepts.a: Internalize that you are more than your successes and simultaneously more than your failures. Feel the real pain of failure without making it your whole identity, which enables purpose-driven next action.
concepts.definition: Resilience practice: internalize that you are more than your successes and simultaneously more than your failures. Failure is real pain ("a deep gash") but accepting it without collapsing identity enables purpose-driven action and deeper appreciation of wins. sridhar ramaswamy after neeva: company less successful than Google tenure — okay; resilience may matter more than uninterrupted success. Teaches children and self this framing; cherishes successes more when you understand how unusual they are. Startup corollary: every startup requires quasi-religious belief (act of madness); failures carry lessons if identity isn't fused to outcome. Artist/athlete parallel (big sean / jay shetty): Denze…

concepts.title: Idiosyncratic Compatibility
concepts.q: What kind of romantic fit shows up when mutual ratings diverge from what the crowd would predict?
concepts.a: Idiosyncratic compatibility: unique shared moments, inside jokes, and co-created stories create fit that consensus attractiveness ratings miss.
concepts.definition: The fit that emerges when two people's ratings of each other diverge from crowd consensus — often built from unique shared moments, inside jokes, and co-created stories rather than universally admired traits.

concepts.title: Impact Communication
concepts.q: Why can high performers stall if they assume excellent work will speak for itself?
concepts.a: Managers see output more than invisible effort, decisions, and prevented fires. Impact communication gives context for value created—without bragging—so reputation catches up to competence.
concepts.definition: Giving context for the value you created without bragging, exaggerating, or making yourself the hero. The failure mode: believing your work should speak for itself — fixing problems quietly, going above and beyond, assuming your boss already knows. Reality: managers are busy with fires you never hear about; people above you often see output, not effort, decisions, or prevention. Engineers preventing outages, PMs removing roadblocks, admins preventing chaos — the work is real but invisible if never communicated. Fix language (5 invisible habits kill career growth): "Here's the part of the results I was able to drive… I'd love to share the impact of the changes I made." Not showing off — closi…

concepts.title: Impossible Problems as Motivation
concepts.q: How can expert claims that something is 'not technically possible' become fuel rather than a stop sign?
concepts.a: For some builders, that rejection is the primary motivator—the only work worth doing. Obsessive pursuit targets problems deemed impossible rather than agreed-easy opportunities.
concepts.definition: When experts say "this is not technically possible" or "nobody can do this," that rejection becomes the primary motivator — the only work worth doing. Obsessive pursuit of problems deemed impossible.

concepts.title: Incident vs Injury Language
concepts.q: Why does Starrett reserve the word 'injury' for only some painful events in training or work?
concepts.a: Injury means clear mechanical trauma needing emergency care (e.g., bone out of place). Tweaks and strains that do not stop function are incidents/non-injuries—language that reduces catastrophizing and guides care level.
concepts.definition: kelly starrett's clinical language precision: injury = clear mechanical trauma (e.g. bone visibly out of place) requiring emergency care. Everything else — tweaks, strains, incidents that don't prevent job function — call a non-injury or incident. Language from Lawrence Gonzalez's Deep Survival: people accumulate "got away with it" patterns until failure; precise labeling reduces fear catastrophizing that worsens movement guarding. Goal: respond appropriately without treating every back tweak as career-ending injury narrative.
