concepts.a: It generates thousands of contract-relax-like patterns linking upper and lower body at speed in positions unsafe under heavy load, adding a velocity readiness component static stretches miss.
concepts.definition: Movement system by David Weck using a climbing rope — generates thousands of pnf contract relax mobility-like patterns linking upper and lower body, wrists, elbows, shoulders at speed in positions that would be vulnerable under heavy load. Used in hip shoulder breath spin up homework for shoulder/neck pain; ~5 minutes produces feel-good readiness with velocity component missing from static warmups. Starrett: foundational piece for athletes with shoulder/neck issues — daily rope flow assignment.

concepts.title: Rough-and-Tumble vs Bullying
concepts.q: When does rough-and-tumble kid play cross the line into bullying?
concepts.a: When it shifts to power-over with no concern for the other person's emotional life—hierarchy without empathy. Healthy physical play and banter stay culturally variable but still care about the other's feelings.
concepts.definition: Rough-and-tumble physical play among kids is fine and culturally variable (touch, handshakes, banter). It becomes bullying when it shifts to power over with no concern for the other person's emotional life — hierarchy-establishing dialogue with zero empathy. Huberman's friend-group experience of natural hierarchy vs Brackett's boundary. Distinct from healthy physical interaction and emotion voicing at a distance.

concepts.title: Routine GitHub Persistence
concepts.q: Why must remote Claude Code routines commit and push memory updates before the cloud instance dies?
concepts.a: They clone into a disposable environment; local file edits vanish when it destroys. Without pushing to the tracked branch, the next run starts from stale repo state.
concepts.definition: Remote Claude Code routines clone a GitHub repo into a disposable cloud environment, run, then destroy the instance. File updates made during a run are lost unless the agent commits and pushes to main (or the tracked branch). Without push-back, the next scheduled run starts from stale repo state — breaking stateless routine memory. Setup requirements: - Private GitHub repo with project files, memory, skills, and routines/ prompts - Allow unrestricted branch pushes in routine permissions (not limited to claude/* branches) - Prompts must instruct: read files → work → commit memory updates → push Local routines run on your machine and persist files directly; remote routines need git as the pers…

concepts.title: RULER & Mood Meter
concepts.q: What do RULER and the Mood Meter train students to do with emotions instead of treating them as nuisances?
concepts.a: RULER teaches Recognize, Understand, Label, Express, Regulate. The Mood Meter maps feelings on energy × pleasantness so emotions get rigorous instruction, not optional wellness fluff.
concepts.definition: RULER — evidence-based social-emotional learning from marc brackett's Yale Center: Recognize, Understand, Label, Express, Regulate emotions. Adopted by 3,500+ schools; Harlem district example (21 schools, thousands of kids) under deputy superintendent who was Brackett's former student. Mood Meter — four-quadrant tool (energy × pleasantness) mapping feelings; How We Feel app. Illustrative moment: boy in blue quadrant (low energy, unpleasant) told Brackett he didn't want to bother him with sadness — emotions treated as nuisance. Goal: rigorous EI instruction, not optional wellness.

concepts.title: Russian Bioregulator Peptides
concepts.q: How are Khavinson bioregulator peptides thought to act differently from typical supplements?
concepts.a: Short peptides bind DNA grooves to open or close chromatin like epigenetic modifiers, originally developed for soldiers and astronauts under circadian and immune stress.
concepts.definition: Bioregulator peptides from Soviet/Russian researcher Dr. Vladimir Khavinson — short (di/tri/tetrapeptides) epigenetic modifiers binding DNA grooves to open/close chromatin (modeled like steroid nuclear receptors). Developed for soldiers, submariners, astronauts facing circadian disruption, immune decline, and accelerated aging appearance.

concepts.title: S-Curve Revenue Layering
concepts.q: Why does smooth external company growth often hide stacked S-curves inside?
concepts.a: Each revenue line accelerates then hits a ceiling; you must layer the next curve years ahead. Waiting until growth stalls is already too late.
concepts.definition: External company growth often looks smooth, but internally it's stacked S-curves — each revenue source accelerates, plateaus at a glass ceiling, then needs the next curve layered in. amit bendov: plan 2–3 years ahead; when you realize you need a new curve, it's already too late. gong response to single-product slowdown: built two additional products (now ~four lines), so external growth resumed while bundled competitors stalled. Pairs with competitor vs customer obsession — innovate new curves vs mirror rival bundles.

concepts.title: SaaS Churn via Product Shipping
concepts.q: How can daily shipping and better onboarding cut SaaS churn better than hiding the cancel button?
concepts.a: Make the product genuinely better and visibly improving—persona onboarding, setup calls, shipping signals—so customers stay because value is clear, not because exit is dark-patterned.
concepts.definition: Reducing churn by making the product genuinely better and making improvements visible — daily shipping signals, persona-based onboarding, human setup calls — rather than cancellation-flow tricks (hiding cancel buttons, dark patterns).

concepts.title: SaaS Growth Phase Transitions
concepts.q: Why do the skills that get a SaaS from $0 to $1M ARR often fail from $1M to $10M?
concepts.a: 0→1 is finding demand and brute-forcing with a small team. 1→10 needs leadership, selling, culture, processes, and team-building—different skills—even if 1→10 skills later transfer toward $100M.
concepts.definition: Distinct problem sets at each ARR scale. 0→$1M: find what people want, talk to customers, brute-force with small team. $1M→$10M: leadership, selling, culture, processes, team-building — different skills. $10M→$100M: another beast, but 1→10 skills transfer.

concepts.title: SAFE Equity Financing
concepts.q: Why use a SAFE when early startup valuation is hard to agree on?
concepts.a: Investors put capital in now at a discount to a future priced round, skipping today's worth fight that kills deals. It also lets employee equity exist without fixing a premature stock price.

concepts.title: SAINT Protocol RTMS
concepts.q: Why might accelerated theta-burst RTMS be preferred over ketamine for treatment-resistant depression?
concepts.a: SAINT delivers strong evidence with sham-controllable sessions over five days, without ketamine's addiction and bladder risks. Plasticity still must be directed, not just opened.
concepts.definition: SAINT protocol (Nolan Williams, Stanford): accelerated theta-burst RTMS to dorsolateral prefrontal cortex — 5 consecutive days, 10 min on / 50 min off cycles. Stronger evidence than ketamine for treatment-resistant depression; sham-controllable (unlike psychedelics where blinding fails). Medicare covers RTMS; scaling limited by expensive machines. Humphreys prefers SAINT over ketamine (addiction + bladder damage risk). New multi-site RTMS trial for cannabis use disorder (Greg Schaeffer). Humphreys hopes more RTMS before invasive implants. Plasticity must be directed, not merely "opened" — Huberman/Lembke caution on undifferentiated plasticity.

concepts.title: Sales AI Asymmetry Complexity
concepts.q: Why does sales remain hard for AI even as models get stronger?
concepts.a: Buyers hide truth, multi-party politics matter, winning moves are creatively unreasonable, and incentives clash. Models are trained to be plausible, not strategically inventive, so data helps but full autonomy does not.
concepts.definition: Why revenue/sales AI remains hard even as models improve — domain features that stress current AI strengths: - Asymmetrical engagement — buyers don't share full truth; may claim budget authority or competitor evaluations they don't have - B2B multi-party politics — multiple sellers to multiple buyers; power dynamics and org politics - Ambiguity and judgment — "reading the room," timing, creative unreasonable moves that win deals but aren't statistically plausible training targets - Negotiation tension — opposing price incentives on both sides amit bendov: AI is trained to be plausible, not strategically creative. Excellent context/data (see crm data capture gap) is necessary but not sufficie…

concepts.title: Sales System (Post-Launch)
concepts.q: After launch, how does an ongoing sales system differ from marketing?
concepts.a: Marketing generates inbound interest; sales is proactive outbound—upsells, surveys, referrals, sponsor pipelines with conversion math. Most founders underbuild this after launch.
concepts.definition: Sales is separate from marketing. Marketing generates inbound/reactive interest; sales is outbound/proactive: upsell existing customers, run surveys, referral codes, sponsor outreach with pipeline math.

concepts.title: Salience and Attention
concepts.q: What is salience in attention, and what does healthy self-function do with it?
concepts.a: Salience is what feels most obvious to attend to among thousands of internal and external candidates. Health means choosing what to prioritize rather than reactive fixation on threat or self-criticism.
concepts.definition: Salience (paul conti): what feels most obvious to attend to — internally (thoughts, feelings) or externally (stimuli). Thousands of candidates compete; healthy function of self includes choosing salience rather than reactive fixation.

concepts.title: Salty-Sweet Taste Interaction
concepts.q: Why do salty-sweet processed foods keep you eating past homeostatic need?
concepts.a: Pure salty or sweet pathways eventually signal stop, but combined they mask each other's satiety signals. Hidden sugars further bypass sweet stopping rules and drive continued craving.
concepts.definition: Parallel neural pathways (Zucker lab, Columbia) represent pure tastes — salty, sweet, bitter, umami — from mouth and gut through brainstem to neocortex. These pathways interact: - Pure salty → homeostatic reduction in salty appetite after sufficient intake. - Pure sweet → threshold where you stop wanting more sugar. - Salty + sweet combined (common in processed foods) → each taste masks the other's satiety signal → continued consumption beyond homeostatic need. Hidden sugars — manufacturers add sugar/artificial sweeteners while disguising sweetness → bypass sweet stopping rules → dopamine-driven craving for more. Practical implication — to find your true salt needs and reduce sugar cravings,…

concepts.title: Samskara Yoga Nidra Trauma
concepts.q: How does Yoga Nidra aim to change trauma-related patterns beyond surface relaxation?
concepts.a: It targets samskaras—learned imprints carried forward like weeds—burning them at the root while using a sankalpa compass for fundamental belief change, not just temporary calm.
concepts.definition: Samskaras — learned imprints carried forward experience-to-experience like learning weeds. Yoga Nidra aims to burn samskaras at the root (relax body, active mind, sleep debt recovery) plus sun gulpa personal compass sankalpa for fundamental belief change.

concepts.title: Sarcopenia / Age-Related Muscle Loss
concepts.q: What happens to muscle after 40 if you stay sedentary, and what does resistance training change?
concepts.a: Sedentary adults lose about 1% muscle mass and 1–3% strength per year. Training largely plateaus the decline so function for stairs, grandkids, and independence is maintained—even if you start late.

concepts.title: Sauna Growth Hormone Protocol
concepts.q: Why should a growth-hormone-oriented sauna protocol be used infrequently rather than daily?
concepts.a: Repeated heat adapts you and blunts the GH spike (huge day 1, much smaller by day 7). For max GH use roughly once a week or every 10 days, fasted, ideally later day to align with overnight GH release.
concepts.definition: Distinct from cardiovascular deliberate heat exposure protocols — optimized for growth hormone (GH) spike, not daily health habit. GH supports metabolism, tissue repair, and fat loss; natural GH release drops sharply after early adulthood (especially during slow-wave sleep early in the night).

concepts.title: Sauna Hot-Cold Cortisol Protocol
concepts.q: What heat-and-cold contrast pattern has evidence for lowering cortisol?
concepts.a: Multiple short high-heat sauna bouts followed by cool water (study: 4×12 min at ~90°C then ~10°C cool-down) significantly decreased cortisol. Cold showers are a more accessible directional substitute.
concepts.definition: Research-backed deliberate heat + cold contrast for cortisol reduction — distinct from cardiovascular frequency protocols in deliberate heat exposure protocols.

concepts.title: Saving vs Investing Framework
concepts.q: How should purpose and risk differ between money you save and money you invest?
concepts.a: Saving prioritizes safety, liquidity, and near-term goals in low-risk accounts. Investing accepts higher risk and less liquidity for medium/long-term growth like retirement—after budgeting and an emergency fund exist.
concepts.definition: Distinction from financial literacy tina huang / khan academy: | | Saving | Investing | |---|-----------|---------------| | Purpose | Safety, liquidity, short-term goals | Growth, medium/long-term goals | | Risk | Very low | Higher (potential loss) | | Access | Easy withdrawal | Less liquid | | Examples | Savings account, HYSA, CDs | Stocks, bonds, index funds, mutual funds | | Use for | Emergency fund, near-term purchases | Retirement, 5+ year goals | Don't stash cash under mattress — bank accounts at minimum; CDs lock money for higher rates.

concepts.title: Scalable Side Hustle vs Time-Trade
concepts.q: Why treat Uber-style hourly side income as temporary while prioritizing scalable skill businesses?
concepts.a: Time-trade pay stays capped per hour forever. Scalable work (YouTube, copy, editing) ties pay to value created and can explode after years of compound practice—better as the long-term path.
concepts.definition: Two categories of extra income (gabe bult): Time-trade (capped) — render service → get paid (Uber Eats, hourly freelancing). Income flat per hour forever; no compounding. Acceptable only short-term (6 months) to fund house hacking fha multifamily, pay off car, or lower freedom number income based — never the end goal. Scalable (uncapped) — skill output scales with mastery; pay tied to value created, not hours traded. Examples: YouTube, premium copywriting leverage, script writing, video editing, channel/social management. Often years at $0 before income "explodes" (compound work effect).

concepts.title: Scarcity Meaning Quality Hiring
concepts.q: In recruiting, why can flooding candidates with AI-driven opportunities destroy meaning?
concepts.a: Meaning comes from scarcity; when everything is abundant, nothing feels special. A few high-fit options beat a thousand generic outreaches that signal low quality.
concepts.definition: In recruiting, meaning comes from scarcity — when candidates are bombarded with opportunities, nothing feels special. john kim: misuse AI → loses meaning, makes things "not special." Special correlates with scarcity and quality over quantity. Product implication: constrained candidate matching (3–4 high-fit options) beats presenting 1,000 choices. Counter-position to AI startups maximizing outbound volume.

concepts.title: Screen Height Alertness
concepts.q: How does screen height relative to your eyes change alertness while working?
concepts.a: Screens at or above eye level engage brainstem alertness via upward gaze. Looking down about 30° with half-closed lids reduces alertness and increases sleepiness—common with low laptops.
concepts.definition: Position your screen at or above eye level (ideally slightly higher). Upward gaze engages brainstem alertness circuits linked to eye muscles and eyelids. Looking down at ~30° with partially closed eyelids reduces alertness and increases sleepiness — a common default for laptop/tablet users.

concepts.title: Second Brain Five Levels
concepts.q: Why isn't a higher second-brain level always better than a simpler one?
concepts.a: Each level answers a different retrieval question—from exact paths to semantic search to graphs to autonomous sync. Higher adds complexity; pick by need, and mix levels per subfolder.
concepts.definition: Nate Herk's progressive framework for AI second-brain architecture in Claude Code (tool-agnostic — works with Codex, Hermes Agent, etc.). Each level answers a different retrieval question; higher levels add complexity, not automatic superiority. | Level | Core mechanism | Question it answers | | ----- | -------------- | ------------------- | | 1 | CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md router + folders | Can I find this by exact word or file path? | | 2 | LLM Wiki + memory.md + references | Can I pull everything on a topic together? | | 3 | Vector / semantic search (Pinecone, Supabase, Obsidian smart lookup) | Can I find it when I use different words than I wrote? | | 4 | Knowledge graph (LightRAG, Logseq, G…

concepts.title: Seduction As Power
concepts.q: How does Robert Greene's warm seduction lower resistance differently than commanding someone?
concepts.a: It creates pleasure and interest so influence happens without the other noticing domination. Overt commands breed resentment; warm courtship is mutual play, not cold extraction.
concepts.definition: Robert Greene defines seduction as a high form of power — you create pleasure, excitement, and interest in another person so their resistance to your ideas lowers without them realizing they are being influenced. Contrasts with yelling, commanding, or overt domination (which breeds resentment). Seduction is also a mating ritual — elaborate across the animal kingdom; modern culture wrongly treats romance as "just be yourself" with zero effort. Warm seduction is a mutual courtship game, not cold extraction (money/sex only). Core seducer move: outer directed seduction — turn off internal monologue ("do they like me?") and enter the other's psychology.

concepts.title: Seed Oils Evidence Debate
concepts.q: Why is blanket seed-oil vilification misleading according to lipid evidence debates?
concepts.a: Oils are over-vilified and animal fats also show harms head-to-head; canola can lower LDL more than olive oil. Context matters—seed oils often ride in ultra-processed foods, confounding oil type alone.
concepts.definition: Social-media seed oil vilification (canola, soybean, etc.) vs evidence-based lipid literature — alan aragon argues both sides oversimplify: seed oils over-vilified; animal fats (butter, tallow, lard) also have adverse-outcome data when compared head-to-head. Canola specifics: - Meta-analysis: canola vs olive oil → canola lowers LDL more (surprising to many) - High omega-3 proportion relative to other seed oils (also contains omega-6) - Hard to find negative canola trials amid positive lipid effects Context > monolith: Seed oils often "ride in the wrong vehicle" — ultra-processed foods with refined starch, added sugar, low protein (highly palatable food reset). Olive oil/grass-fed butter corr…

concepts.title: Seeing vs Noticing Memory
concepts.q: Why can you look at a fire extinguisher for years and still not know where it is?
concepts.a: Habituation: seeing is passive retinal exposure without encoding. Noticing requires engaged search—finding it yourself builds durable location memory that pass-bys never create.
concepts.definition: Habituation means repeated visual exposure without encoding: retinas receive the fire extinguisher for years; conscious memory never stores its location. alan castel contrast: seeing (passive pass-by) vs noticing (engaged search). Protocol — stand up and find the extinguisher yourself rather than be told — forces environment engagement and durable location memory. Parallel demos: Apple logo / penny features invisible despite thousands of views until you must reconstruct them.

concepts.title: Selective Memory Aging
concepts.q: How can becoming more selective about what to remember be adaptive with age?
concepts.a: Limited capacity plus wisdom means allocating memory to what matters rather than learning everything. Pair with selective optimization—keep valued activities instead of a full youthful load.
concepts.definition: With age, people often become more selective about what they try to learn and remember — less "learn everything," more focus on valued domains. alan castel treats this as adaptive: metacognition + wisdom let you allocate limited capacity to what matters. Paired with Baltes-style selective optimization with compensation — keep the one hike you love weekly rather than the full youthful activity set. Not pure decline narrative: some systems worsen (name interference), others stabilize/improve (verbal knowledge, prioritization).

concepts.title: Self-Centric Shyness
concepts.q: Why is shyness better framed as a focus problem than a fixed personality flaw?
concepts.a: The inner loop stays on me-me-me deficits instead of the other person, consuming bandwidth for listening and spontaneity. Shifting attention outward breaks the self-reinforcing performance drop.
concepts.definition: The core psychological driver of shyness: thinking too much about yourself during social interaction. Inner monologue loops on deficits — "I'm not interesting enough," "no one wants to talk to me," "I'm not funny," "what if I forget and look stupid" — instead of engaging with the other person or the moment. Vinh Giang frames this as tough to admit because it feels like a character flaw, but it's a focus problem, not a fixed trait. The thoughts share one pattern: me, me, me.

concepts.title: Semantic Search — When to Use (Level 3)
concepts.q: When is vector semantic search a poor fit even if your notes corpus is large?
concepts.a: Whole-document summaries and aggregate analytics need full context or scans across all data; chunking returns partial hits. Use vectors for needle-in-haystack meaning matches with different vocabulary.
concepts.definition: Level 3 second-brain retrieval: chunk documents → embed with an embeddings model → store in a vector DB (Pinecone, Supabase, Qdrant, Obsidian smart lookup). Search by meaning similarity ("X is like Y, Z") rather than exact keyword match ("X equals X"). Good fit: - Large text corpora where you need a specific needle ("remind me what rule 17 was" in 1,000 rules) - Queries use different vocabulary than source text ("feedback" → matches "evaluations", "live test results") Poor fit: - Whole-document tasks ("summarize the March 5 meeting") — chunking returns partial context, misses key sections - Aggregate/analytical questions ("which week had highest sales?") — vector search may grab one high-sim…

concepts.title: Sense Organs False Desires
concepts.q: Why are desires triggered by senses and social comparison often unreliable guides to what people truly want?
concepts.a: Sense organs and comparison—especially via social media—generate wants that mismatch deeper needs. Psychiatry's hard work is revealing that gap.
concepts.definition: Desires triggered by sense organs and comparison (especially social media) often aren't what people truly want — psychiatry's hardest task is revealing the mismatch.

concepts.title: Sentence Energy
concepts.q: Why can clear articulation still sound weak if vocal energy drops mid-sentence?
concepts.a: Articulation is mouth precision; sentence energy is full vitality from first word to last. Trailing into fry or low volume kills presence even when syllables are correct.
concepts.definition: Maintaining full vocal vitality from the first word to the last of a sentence — not trailing off into low volume, crackly vocal fry, or flat delivery. Vinh Giang separates this from over articulation (mouth precision): you can articulate syllables correctly but still sound weak if motor volume drops mid-sentence. Count-to-10 drill: Count aloud 1 through 10 with identical energy on every number — trains consistent presence before applying to full questions or statements. Application: Re-ask the same question or deliver the same content with the entire utterance energized — the student demo shows crisp, non-nasal speech only after both articulation and energy are addressed. Low-presence speech…

concepts.title: Serotonin, Social Connection & Long-Term Stress
concepts.q: Beyond sleep and exercise, what long-term stress buffers work through serotonin?
concepts.a: Genuine in-person connection and delight/play—not digital proxies. Recognizing trusted people triggers serotonin that supports immunity, repair, and lasting neural connection.
concepts.definition: The strongest long-term chronic stress mitigators (beyond exercise, sleep, and real-time tools) are genuine social connection and experiences of delight/play — not digital proxies. Serotonin is a neuromodulator that biases brain/body circuits toward well-being. At high levels → bliss/contentment; at moderate levels → "enough in the immediate environment." Recognizing someone you trust triggers serotonin release → positive effects on immune system, neural repair, and synaptic maintenance (prevents long-term connection "withering"). Connection forms: romantic, familial, friendship, pets, or delight in anything — art, nature, play. Requires investment, time, and tolerating imperfection in relat…

concepts.title: Service-Maxxing Golden Rules
concepts.q: What three business-value tests should a digital service pass to be worth selling toward $10K/month?
concepts.a: It must make a business more money, save it time, or save it money. Those beats passive-income schemes or commodity task work.
concepts.definition: Service-maxxing (mark tilbury) means choosing a digital service that passes at least one of three golden rules for business value: 1. Make a business more money 2. Save a business time 3. Save a business money The fastest path to $10K/month online is selling such a service — not passive income schemes or commodity task work.

concepts.title: Serving Capacity (Model Usability)
concepts.q: Why can a high-quality model still be useless in real workflows?
concepts.a: Practical usefulness depends on serving speed and availability, not only weights. Throttling, frozen subscriptions, or closed weights can make a great model unusable when demand exceeds compute.
concepts.definition: A model’s practical usefulness is gated by whether the provider can serve it at acceptable speed and availability — not only by weights quality. If demand exceeds compute, subscription freezes, throttling, or closed weights can make a “great” model unusable in real workflows.

concepts.title: Session Cost Movement
concepts.q: Why track the mobility trade-offs of each training modality like vital signs?
concepts.a: Heavy cycling can tighten hips; elliptical may skip needed hip extension. Knowing session cost lets you add corrective work so restrictions don't surprise you later.
concepts.definition: Awareness that each training modality carries mobility trade-offs — like vital signs for movement. kelly starrett example: heavy cycling/mountain biking tightens hips despite cardiovascular benefit; elliptical may skip hip extension demands that running or squatting provide. Track session cost the way you'd track blood pressure (120/80 baseline): if you ride bikes a ton, add hip-opening work to the movement diet. Prevents surprise restrictions when switching modalities or aging.

concepts.title: Session Handoff
concepts.q: Why prefer a structured session handoff plus clear over compacting a long Claude Code chat?
concepts.a: Handoff captures decisions, files, state, and next steps for a fresh window without slow compact. It resets context rot, preserves awareness, and avoids breaking prompt cache the way compact does.
concepts.definition: A custom Claude Code skill (/session-handoff) that replaces reliance on /compact for managing context rot. Workflow: 1. Run /session-handoff before clearing context 2. Skill outputs structured summary: where started, locked decisions, what shipped, key files, running state, verification status, deferred/open questions, pick up here 3. /copy the handoff → /clear → paste back 4. Continue in a fresh window with full situational awareness Enables cross-tool transfer (e.g. handoff to Codex or another model). New session may confirm running state before recommending next move. Nate prefers this over /compact (slow) and autocompact. Pairs with status line monitoring and /context to audit token usag…

concepts.title: Settle for Pain of Where You Are
concepts.q: Why do people stay stuck even when not pursuing a dream also hurts?
concepts.a: Pursuit looks painful, so they settle for the familiar pain of where they are—or aim for mediocrity to avoid unmet expectations. Cure is small daily steps toward the desire, not suppressing it.
concepts.definition: jay shetty / mel robbins frame: people feel the pain of not going after what they want (calling, need, dream) because pursuing looks painful — so they settle for the pain of where they already are. Related trap: treat life as binary (dreams-or-stuck-forever) and choose stuck because the dream seems far; or adopt "strive for mediocrity" so expectations can't be unmet (Robbins: worst advice). Robbins's cure metaphor: a treatable cancer you'd treat — the burning flame of desire won't go out; suppressing it via campaign of misery creates worse pain. Only cure: stop listening to the campaign and take one small step a day toward the thing. Shetty's parallel: leave safe corporate path after looking…
keywords: Pain of Staying vs Pain of Pursuing

concepts.title: Seven and Eight Hiring Rule
concepts.q: Why are solid but mediocre 7s and 8s more dangerous to keep than obvious misfits?
concepts.a: They surf without consequences, so 9s and 10s leave—why work hard if slack is tolerated? Help or re-role first; if wrong fit, fire to protect A-player culture.

concepts.title: Sexual Health Social Contagion
concepts.q: How can peer norms change sexual health behavior without any biochemical contagion?
concepts.a: Groups joking that intimacy is dead normalize decline; positive circles raise standards. A large intervention found ~46% more safe/positive sexual behaviors when surrounded by healthier influences.
concepts.definition: Peer groups normalize (or demoralize) sexual priorities — parallel to social contagion networks in career/health contexts: - Physician friend groups joking "we never have sex anymore" → normalizes decline - Friends who vent negatively about partners vs celebrate intimacy shape your standards - Intervention study (~50K people): Surrounding self with positive sexual-health influences → 46% increase in safe/positive sexual behaviors Malik's husband's physician peer group vs choosing friends who value partners positively. Distinct from cortisol stress contagion (biochemical) — this is normative/attitudinal contagion.

concepts.title: Sexual Marketplace Top Ten Percent
concepts.q: In a deregulated sexual marketplace, what happens when many freely choose top-tier partners?
concepts.a: Roughly the top 10% of men capture disproportionate options across cultures and species patterns, creating scarcity for others and low odds any top man gives up optionality for monogamy unless timing aligns.
concepts.definition: End state of a deregulated sexual marketplace: top ~10% of men capture disproportionate sex — "what a time to be alive" for them. Pattern seen across species (elephant seals, wild mustangs): alpha males dominate; females aren't loyal after defeat — they move to the next winner. Historical: ~85% of cultures polygamous; when women choose freely, many target top-tier men — creating problems for both sexes. Women face negligible odds any top-10% man gives up extreme optionality for monogamy unless timing catches him ready to settle ("good enough at the right moment," not necessarily "the right woman"). Alternative — "completely average, nothing bad nothing great, great husband" — sounds unattrac…

concepts.title: SHBG & Free Testosterone Balance
concepts.q: Why can total testosterone look fine while free androgen action still feels low?
concepts.a: SHBG binds most sex hormones; only ~1–2% free T is bioavailable. High SHBG (e.g. in lean deficits) can leave free T low even when total looks normal.
concepts.definition: Sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG) — plasma protein binding all androgens and estrogens; stronger androgens bind more tightly. Free testosterone (~1–2% of total) is bioavailable for receptor action. Key dynamics: - High SHBG → more total DHT bound but less free DHT; need balance of total vs free androgens - DHT (dihydrotestosterone) — strongest bioidentical androgen; drives secondary sexual characteristics especially during puberty - Caloric deficit in lean individuals → SHBG rises → free androgens/estrogens fall - Very high SHBG + low free T (e.g., free T of 2) may require different TRT dosing even with "normal" total T Interventions: - Boron 5–12 mg/day — acutely lowers SHBG (not sustaine…

concepts.title: Shell vs Core Temperature Regulation
concepts.q: Why must heat and cold protocols respect shell vs core temperature separately?
concepts.a: Skin shell and internal core are distinct; the brain compares shell to decide heating or cooling. Misdesigned heat can tip core into hyperthermia where CNS damage is irreversible.
concepts.definition: At all times the body maintains two distinct temperatures: the shell (skin surface) and the core (viscera, nervous system, spinal cord). The brain continuously compares shell temperature to decide whether to heat up or cool down — essential for designing deliberate heat exposure protocols that target goals without crossing into hyperthermia (CNS neuron damage is irreversible; safe heat window is narrower than safe cold).

concepts.title: Short Squeeze Mechanics
concepts.q: In a short squeeze, what feedback loop forces prices higher?
concepts.a: Shorts must buy shares to cover; rising prices force more covering buys that push price further up. Losses transfer from covering shorts to sellers who exit near the top.
concepts.definition: short squeeze mechanics is the market mechanism where short sellers (who borrowed and sold shares betting on a price drop) are forced to buy shares back to close positions, pushing price up — ideally for shorts, they buy back lower than they sold. In a squeeze, rising price forces covering buys that further raise price. Money transferred in a classic squeeze flows from short sellers buying overpriced shares to sellers who exit at the top.

concepts.title: Show Yourself
concepts.q: Why put unfinished work and demos in public before you feel ready?
concepts.a: Visibility beats perfection. Proof artifacts—posts, talks, videos—answer what have you built with links, not claims.
concepts.definition: Alex Hormozi's advice: put yourself out there before you feel ready. Visibility beats perfection — speak, post, record builds, share demos. The goal is proof artifacts (videos, talks, LinkedIn posts) so when someone asks "what have you built?" you have links, not claims.

concepts.title: Shunya Void Meditation
concepts.q: What remains the object of shunya meditation after stripping positive and negative self-labels?
concepts.a: Contact with what remains beyond roles and judgments (husband, loser, successful)—void beyond attributes—useful when working with narcissism-pattern identity clinging.
concepts.definition: Meditation on shunya (void): strip all positive and negative self-attributes (husband, doctor, loser, successful) to contact what remains beyond labels — recommended for narcissism-pattern work.

concepts.title: Shutdown Ritual Productivity
concepts.q: How does a fixed end-of-work shutdown ritual protect evenings better than just stopping when tired?
concepts.a: Close open loops to an external system, preview tomorrow briefly, then use a demonstrative phrase or gesture of closure. That conditions work thoughts to stay at work, like not associating bed with wakefulness.
concepts.definition: Cal Newport's #3 protocol: a fixed end-of-work routine that clearly demarcates work from personal/evening time. Steps: (1) close open loops — review inbox, plan, time-blocked calendar; ensure nothing urgent missed; capture anything still in head to external system; (2) preview tomorrow — not full plan, but sense of next day; (3) demonstrative closure — phrase or gesture signaling "shutdown complete" (Newport uses verbal phrase). Matt Walker parallel (cited): associating bed with wakefulness ruins sleep; associating dinner table with work thoughts ruins relationships — cognitive context conditioning applies to work boundaries too.

concepts.title: Shy-to-Confident Bridge
concepts.q: Why practice looking and sounding confident before you feel confident inside?
concepts.a: You cannot jump from years of shyness to felt certainty in one step. Outward behaviors others read as confidence form a bridge until inner certainty catches up.
concepts.definition: Vinh Giang's staged model for confidence growth: you don't jump from 20 years of shyness to felt confidence in one step. The bridge is learning to not look shy and not sound shy — adopting outward behaviors (posture, open body language, eye contact, volume, lower pitch endings, pauses) that others read as confidence before inner certainty catches up. Vinh's early stage example: sweating through shirts hidden under black vest and shirt, panicking internally — yet appearing calm enough that an attendee said he seemed confident on one of his first times on stage.

concepts.title: Sickness Behavior & Immune Signaling
concepts.q: Why is sickness behavior considered a motivated immune strategy rather than mere laziness?
concepts.a: Lethargy, anorexia, irritability, fever, photophobia, and daytime sleep drive conserve energy and fight infection. Fast vagus signals and slower cytokine paths coordinate the suite.
concepts.definition: Sickness behavior is a motivated suite of responses when ill — designed to fight infection, not mere laziness: - Lethargy / reduced activity tolerance - Loss of grooming (cosmetic self-care, animal licking) - Loss of appetite - Irritability / desire to be left alone - Fever — hypothalamic preoptic neurons raise body temperature to kill heat-sensitive pathogens - Photophobia — bright light aversion via eye → anterior thalamus → meninges pathway - Daytime sleep drive — hypothalamic nuclei promote sleep during normal active circadian phase Fast pathway: Vagus nerve (10th cranial nerve) carries infection signals from organs (lungs, heart, gut) to hypothalamus. The vagus is not primarily a "calm-…

concepts.title: Side Hustle Rating Framework (Tilbury)
concepts.q: What three axes should you score to compare side hustles objectively?
concepts.a: Time to learn, money needed, and difficulty reaching $1K/month—each 1–5 with lower better. That beats listing generic ideas without comparable tradeoffs.
concepts.definition: mark tilbury's three-axis scoring system (1–5 each) for comparing side hustles objectively instead of listing generic ideas: | Axis | 1 = best | 5 = worst | | --- | --- | --- | | Time to learn | Quick to become competent | Months/years of mastery | | Money needed | Phone + internet only | Significant startup capital | | $1K/month difficulty | Easy first milestone | Hard to reach $1K/mo | Lower scores are better on all three axes. Used in seven side hustles students rich mark tilbury for all seven ideas with real case studies.

concepts.title: Simple Language (Speaking)
concepts.q: How can choosing plain words improve speaking fluency under pressure?
concepts.a: Rare or jargon words force retrieval pauses that mimic stutter or um-filling. Simple vocabulary lives on faster paths so mouth keeps pace with mind.
concepts.definition: Choosing plain, accessible vocabulary over jargon and complex words to reduce cognitive strain while speaking. Vinh Giang's stuttering video frames this as skill #2: when the brain hunts for a rare or technical word, the search pause mimics stutter — some people fill with "um/ah," others block entirely. Mechanism: Simple words live in faster retrieval paths → mouth keeps pace with mind → fewer fluency breaks. Practice: Vinh claims he rarely uses complicated words in live speech; simplicity is a deliberate fluency strategy, not dumbing down. Pairs with communication frameworks for structure and understand not memorize for depth — expertise does not require ornate vocabulary at the moment of s…

concepts.title: Simulating the World Superpower
concepts.q: Why is mentally simulating problems through others' eyes a core entrepreneurial skill?
concepts.a: You cannot create value without seeing stakeholders' perspectives and playing scenarios forward. Use research and AI to build that world model—not to write the report for you.
concepts.definition: Entrepreneurial and relational superpower: visualize problems through others' eyes via empathy and research — play scenarios forward mentally ("what would happen if I did this?"). po shen loh spends significant life effort improving world-simulation ability; uses AI as research tool, not report writer. Example: Nashville bar singer → asked AI for links/background on Broadway performance competition → built internal logic for country-music performance economics while scouting professional entertainers. research verify chatgpt workflow in service of empathy, not delegation. You cannot solve problems without seeing them from stakeholders' perspectives; cannot create value without interacting wi…

concepts.title: Singular Eye Contact
concepts.q: In group speaking, why hold one person's gaze for a full thought instead of scanning the room?
concepts.a: One person, one idea, then move reads as composed and personal. Fast darting, robotic left-right scans, or staring at one face look shifty, unnatural, or intense.
concepts.definition: The group-speaking rule: hold one person's gaze for the length of a sentence or single idea, then move to someone else — so it feels like you're speaking to one person, not the entire room. Don't: - Dart eyes too fast (shifty) - Scan left-right robotically - Follow fixed A→B→C→A→B→C loops (unnatural) - Look over people's heads - Fixate on one person (staring) Do: One person, one thought, then move. Instantly reads as composed, believable, and worth listening to. Pairs with triangle eye contact technique for one-on-one comfort; this is the group protocol.

concepts.title: Six-figure lifestyle business
concepts.q: How does a six-figure lifestyle business differ from chasing a unicorn exit?
concepts.a: It optimizes fun, freedom, flexibility, fulfillment, and roughly £100K–£300K+ profit on your terms—not hypergrowth. Mild entrepreneurial success for most; scaling past ~$5–10M usually needs a different model.

concepts.title: Skill Creator
concepts.q: What does Anthropic's skill-creator plugin automate when turning an SOP into a Claude Code skill?
concepts.a: From plain English it drafts SKILL.md, tests, iterates, and packages a reusable workflow—removing manual formatting and flaky first attempts. Install globally so any project can invoke it.
concepts.definition: Official Anthropic plugin (skill-creator@claude-plugins-official) that builds other claude code skills from plain-English descriptions. Claude drafts SKILL.md, tests the skill, iterates, and packages a reusable workflow. Can ingest an existing SOP and convert it. Removes manual formatting, trigger-condition guesswork, and flaky first attempts. Nate Herk installs it globally at user scope so any project can invoke skill creation without thinking about it.

concepts.title: Skill-Maxxing Loop
concepts.q: How does skill-maxxing reject learn-first-earn-later when building a service?
concepts.a: Loop define → sell before ready → deliver under pressure → fix bottlenecks → systemize reliability. Learning and earning happen together; rejections are data.
concepts.definition: Skill-maxxing (mark tilbury) rejects "learn first, earn later." Instead, run a repeating five-step loop — learning and earning simultaneously: 1. Define — Strip service to one clear deliverable ("edit videos for businesses," not "become a professional videographer") 2. Sell — Get in front of clients before feeling ready; rejections are data (99% no → 1% yes) 3. Deliver — Paying clients create real pressure; skill builds under deadline and feedback 4. Fix — After several deliveries, use AI to remove repetitive bottlenecks (not full automation prematurely) 5. Systemize — Reliability (on-time, clear comms, invoicing) beats raw talent for repeat work and referrals Each full loop improves definit…

concepts.title: Skill Pyramid (Tilbury)
concepts.q: Why can mastering one service skill still leave you stuck below $10K/month income?
concepts.a: You need all four levers together: positioning upmarket to businesses, ROI-based leverage pricing, reach that pulls inbound demand, and automation/reliability. Miss one and you stay grinding at the bottom.
concepts.definition: mark tilbury's four-element model for turning an online service into a $10,000 income. All four must be mastered together — miss one and you stay at the bottom working hard without breaking through: 1. Positioning — Target businesses that can afford to pay for revenue outcomes, not individuals or low-budget creators. Reposition upmarket: edit for entrepreneurs who sell products (not hobby YouTubers); design thumbnails for established creators; write copy for landing-page conversion (not cheap articles). Some industries simply have more budget. 2. Leverage — Stop billing hours; bill return on investment. Facebook ads charged per revenue generated; social media management priced on engagement …

concepts.title: Sleep & Mitochondrial Restoration
concepts.q: Why can't you out-eat sleep deprivation if the goal is growth and repair?
concepts.a: Sleep is a hypometabolic state that saves ~10–15% daily energy by slowing heart rate and sympathetic tone, reallocating budget to growth, maintenance, and repair. Food cannot replace that reallocation.
concepts.definition: Sleep is a hypometabolic restorative state saving ~10–15% daily energy expenditure by slowing heart rate, temperature, and sympathetic tone — reallocating budget from stress costs toward GMR (growth, maintenance, repair). You cannot out-eat sleep deprivation for vitality.

concepts.title: Sleep Temperature Protocol
concepts.q: Why can a hot bath or sauna before bed help you fall asleep faster?
concepts.a: Sleep onset needs a 1–3°F core drop. Heat then exit accelerates cooling afterward; cold right before bed can raise core and impair sleep.
concepts.definition: Falling asleep requires a 1–3°F core temperature drop after the afternoon peak. Paradoxically, hot bath, hot shower, or sauna before bed accelerates cooling mechanisms once you exit, shortening sleep onset latency — POA-driven cooling continues after leaving heat (shell core temperature regulation). Evening/later-day sauna is preferred over morning for sleep; brief warm/cool rinse post-sauna, then bed. Cold before bed can raise core temperature and impair sleep (exceptions: exhausted individuals). For sauna growth hormone protocol, evening timing + no food 2–3 hr prior aligns GH release with early-night slow-wave sleep. Sleep environment: very dark and cool room. Nighttime cooling also uses …

concepts.title: Sleep & Testosterone Link
concepts.q: How strongly can a week of short sleep hit testosterone versus aging?
concepts.a: About 5 hours vs 8 hours for a week can drop testosterone ~15%—roughly a decade of aging. Treating sleep apnea can raise T substantially because nocturnal production peaks overnight.
concepts.definition: Sleep deprivation directly tanks androgen production: - 5h vs 8h sleep (same man, ~1 week): Testosterone drops ~15% — equivalent to ~10 years of aging - Sleep apnea: Neck circumference >17" (men) or >16" (women) suggests risk; treating apnea can raise testosterone ~200 ng/dL - Mechanism: Nocturnal testosterone production peaks overnight → morning erections/desire correlate Low-T symptoms constellation: fatigue, brain fog, depression, low desire, ED, increased fat / decreased muscle — not ED alone.

concepts.title: Slow Accumulation Attraction
concepts.q: Why do lasting relationships often start middling rather than with fireworks?
concepts.a: Attraction accumulates through repeated evidence—humor, listening, specific heat—until desire stabilizes. Instant spark is not required for bonds that last.
concepts.definition: Attraction that builds gradually through repeated interactions rather than instant "spark." Typical first impressions in relationships that last are middling — "seemed all right," not fireworks — followed by accumulating evidence ("with me, he's funny / a good listener / hot when he does X") until desire stabilizes.

concepts.title: Slow Productivity
concepts.q: What three priorities define Cal Newport's slow productivity against pseudo-productivity?
concepts.a: Do fewer things, work at a natural pace, and obsess over quality. Protect deep output over looking busy with visible activity.
concepts.definition: Cal Newport's framework (Slow Productivity, 2024): the lost art of accomplishment without burnout. Counter to pseudo productivity — do fewer things, work at a natural pace, obsess over quality. Principles include reducing commitments, extending timelines, and protecting deep work protocols output over visible activity. Huberman reading at time of episode; complements rather than replaces circadian/ultradian scheduling from maximize productivity daily protocol huberman.

concepts.title: Small Moments Connection Wellbeing
concepts.q: Why frame wellbeing as pumping a leaky tire with small daily moments rather than chasing peak trips?
concepts.a: Rare peaks fade via hedonic adaptation. Brief genuine connections—Uber talks, compliments, stranger fist bumps—add air continuously; a good life is strings of such moments.
concepts.definition: Nick Epley's reframe of happiness: well-being is not primarily about rare peak experiences (desert trips, weddings) that fade via hedonic adaptation — it's like a leaky tire you keep pumping with small positive moments throughout the day. Mechanism: A 23-minute Uber ride can be boring or deeply meaningful (Iranian driver sharing son killed in protest — mutual tears). Grocery checkout, passing compliments ("killer hat"), fist bumps with strangers, Minor Threat shirt shout across a boardwalk — each adds air to the tire. A good day = string of good moments; good week/month/life = string of days with moments. Not about lasting relationships: Many best moments require no follow-up contact or exch…

concepts.title: SMART Financial Goals
concepts.q: What turns vague money wishes into usable financial goals?
concepts.a: Make them Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Realistic, and Time-bound—e.g. save $100,000 for a house by 30—not I want to be rich.
concepts.definition: Goal-setting framework from khan academy / financial literacy tina huang: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Realistic, Time-bound. Bad: "I want to be rich." Good: "By age 30, save $100,000 down payment for a house."

concepts.title: Smartphone Attention Erosion
concepts.q: How does phone context-switching erode focus after you put the device down?
concepts.a: Rapid in-screen switching trains high-turnover attention that sticks when you return to physical tasks, creating ADHD-like deficits. Tight daily limits help preserve sustained focus capacity.
concepts.definition: Smartphones capture full-device attention while rapidly switching contexts inside the screen (infinite scrolling tabs of content) — brain struggles to leave that high-turnover mode when returning to physical-world tasks requiring sustained aperture control. Result: induced subclinical ADHD-like deficits in work, school, and relationship focus — even without formal ADHD diagnosis. Huberman limits (non-prescriptive guidance): - Adolescents: ≤ 60 minutes/day - Adults: ≤ 2 hours/day Among the best behavioral interventions for preserving focus capacity. Success in most life domains scales with focus brought to the activity. Cross-link improve focus decrease anxiety influence vs concern andy stump…

concepts.title: SNI Framework
concepts.q: How does Spot-Name-Impact turn vague appreciation into useful words of affirmation?
concepts.a: Spot what they did, Name the trait or behavior, state the Impact on you or the relationship. Specific sincere praise beats silent hoping they know.
concepts.definition: Vinh Giang's on-the-spot scaffold for words of affirmation (five love languages): deliver specific, sincere praise instead of staying silent and hoping they know you appreciate them. | Step | Meaning | Example | | ---- | ------- | ------- | | S — Spot | Notice what they did or how they showed up | "When you checked in on me last week…" | | N — Name | Name the specific behavior or trait | "…your kindness and your patience…" | | I — Impact | Say how it helped you, the team, or the relationship | "…it really made me feel like I wasn't alone. I really appreciate it." | Use regularly in everyday moments — not just big occasions. Pairs with communication frameworks as a relational (not just profes…

concepts.title: Snowball Risk Taking Entrepreneurship
concepts.q: Why does entrepreneurial confidence often come from snowballing small public risks rather than genius?
concepts.a: Downsides are usually smaller than feared; each risk (build privately → post → attach name → larger stakes) forces you into positions where you have to make it. Calculated series beat waiting for transformation.
concepts.definition: roy lee's only universal advice: take bigger risks. Downside is usually smaller than feared; upside larger. Risks start small and snowball: build tool privately → post free online → associate publicly → post confidential documents. Five months ago he wanted a Big Tech job; conviction shift came from forced positions where you "have to make it." Confidence gap between Mark Zuckerberg and average human is smaller than it appears — well-calculated risk series, not genius transformation.

concepts.title: Søberg Principle
concepts.q: For cold exposure aimed at brown-fat benefits, why finish on cold and allow shivering?
concepts.a: Ending warm blunts the metabolic signal. Shivering plus norepinephrine helps convert white fat toward beige/brown thermogenic tissue; studies used about 11+ minutes cold weekly.
concepts.definition: Named by andrew huberman after Dr. Susanna Søberg (first author): for metabolic / brown-fat benefits from deliberate cold, finish the session on cold and reach a point of shivering — during immersion or immediately after exit — rather than warming up right away. Study basis: Winter-swimming men, neck-deep immersion, ≥11 minutes total per week (often 2–4 sessions) → enhanced cold-induced thermogenesis and brown-fat stores (Søberg et al., altered brown fat thermoregulation). Mechanism sketch: Shivering in muscle + norepinephrine signaling promotes white fat → beige/brown fat conversion (thermogenic adipocytes around clavicles in adults). Ending on cold preserves the metabolic signal; immediate…

concepts.title: Social Anxiety Misplaced Beliefs
concepts.q: Why does exposure therapy reduce social anxiety more by updating beliefs than by thick-skin numbness?
concepts.a: Fear of rejection is usually wildly misplaced; real tests show more warmth than expected. Correcting those predictions—not just dulling nerves—is the active ingredient.
concepts.definition: The core mechanism behind most social anxiety: fear of rejection and negative judgment is usually wildly misplaced. People hold inaccurate beliefs about how strangers, acquaintances, and even conversation partners will respond — predicting harsh rejection, creepiness labels, or indifference when data show warmth, compliance, and interest far exceed expectations. Fix mechanism: real exposure therapy social anxiety works not primarily by dulling anxiety circuits but by updating beliefs about what other people are like. If you never test pessimistic predictions, they never get corrected — and you miss wonderful people (Epley's Uber driver, train rider Gustavo, plane seatmate Brian). Contrast wi…

concepts.title: Social Connection as Invitation
concepts.q: How does framing a hello as an invitation rather than a demand lower social fear?
concepts.a: You offer a choice to engage; they can decline without conflict and you are not entitled to a response. That reduces imposition fear and encourages testing ambiguous cues.
concepts.definition: Reframe for initiating contact: reaching out is an invitation or opportunity to connect — not a demand. "Hi, I'm Nick" offers the other person a choice to engage; they can keep earbuds in or pull one out. Why it matters: Reduces fear of imposition on both sides. Addresses modern impasse — fear of being creepy vs fear of unwanted approaches — by lowering stakes. Recipient can decline without conflict; giver isn't entitled to response. Testing ambiguous cues: Earbuds + phone might mean isolation from silence, not rejection of you specifically. Invitation frame encourages testing beliefs (Thibault the stern commuter → years of friendship after "Hi, I'm Nick"). Sensitivity required: Read respons…

concepts.title: Social Contagion Networks
concepts.q: Beyond stress hormones, how do behaviors like divorce or obesity spread socially?
concepts.a: Attitudes and habits cluster in networks—divorce and obesity likelihood rise with peer occurrence. Curate your tribe for values you want to absorb without abandoning people in acute crisis.
concepts.definition: Behaviors and psychological states spread through social networks — not just stress (cortisol stress contagion). Statistics cited by tara swart: if someone in your group divorces, you're more likely to divorce within a year; obesity similarly clusters. Tribe curation — intentionally limit who is in your inner circle based on attitudinal contagion: kindness, generosity, openness to growth, mental-health awareness, spiritual curiosity. You tend to meet people at a similar psychological level — both evolution and wound depth. Not about abandoning friends in crisis, but avoiding chronic exposure to values you don't want to absorb.

concepts.title: Social Contract & Civilization
concepts.q: Why use maternal mortality as a proxy for civilizational social-contract health?
concepts.a: It reflects mutual obligation to protect neighbors. The US rate is far worse than peer rich countries, signaling medical inequality and an eroding contract versus cold profit-maximization.
concepts.definition: jeremy grantham's measure of civilizational health: social contract — mutual expectation to behave responsibly toward neighbors/community (Japan strong; US eroding toward "screw everybody else"). Proxy metric: maternal mortality per 100K births: | Country | Rate (approx.) | |---------|----------------| | Norway | ~0 | | Sweden | ~2.1 | | Germany | ~4 | | UK | ~5 | | US (all) | ~20–21 | | US (Black) | ~44 | | Nigeria | ~480 | US = 50% worse than next-worst rich country — sign of medical inequality and broken contract. Corporate community obligation (stadiums, local investment) replaced by cold global profit-maximization. Country recommendations for safety nets, life expectancy, murder rates: …

concepts.title: Social Entrepreneurship Win-Win Ecosystem
concepts.q: What makes a social-entrepreneurship ecosystem win-win rather than zero-sum?
concepts.a: Every participant's pain point is served—e.g. kids learn thinking, teen teachers polish skill, artists earn flexible income—so value multiplies instead of extracting from one side.
concepts.definition: Business model where every participant genuinely wins — not zero-sum extraction. po shen loh's live math ecosystem aligns three pain points: (1) middle schoolers who need to learn how to think, (2) mathematically strong high school students who become extraordinarily polished through teaching, (3) drama/improv professionals (Broadway/Hollywood quality) who need flexible part-time income to support their passions. Insight chain: improv classes improved Loh's communication → drama department had talented coaches seeking part-time work → pairing with math geniuses who smile solves the "expert but unfriendly" failure mode. Parent justification rule: no teen task unless Loh could explain to their…

concepts.title: Social Exposure Ladder
concepts.q: How should you progress social exposure from greetings to harder stranger interactions?
concepts.a: Normalize each level before advancing: greet with eye contact and smile, then add genuine compliments, then longer conversations. Reps reduce trigger intensity over time.
concepts.definition: Vinh Giang's progressive exposure therapy ladder for desensitizing social nerves — parallel to presentation desensitization ladder but for everyday stranger interactions. Thesis: high stress/anxiety/blank-mind in social situations means you barely do that thing; reps decrease the trigger over time (window cleaners: 23 years → "it's just a Wednesday").

concepts.title: Social Media Addiction Norms
concepts.q: Why can't individual willpower alone solve social-media addiction coordination problems?
concepts.a: Users hate platforms but stay because everyone else is on them. Norms (no phones at dinner), lockboxes, and collective rules work like don't-drink-and-drive—medicine alone is insufficient.
concepts.definition: Social media addiction rising (phone, games, porn, gambling apps) — 5-year natural history unknown (new phenomenon). Coordination problem: users hate platforms but "everyone else is on it"; WTA study — same people pay to leave if others stay, pay to stay if others leave. Individual tools: second phone in timed lockbox (Huberman), dumb phone, notification-suppression apps requiring code, Australian teen ban experiment (collective not individual exile). Norms needed (like "don't drink before noon"): no social media at dinner/restaurant; can't solve by clinical medicine alone. Compare evolving don't drink and drive norm. Success stories: Dopamine Nation + reward-circuitry education + extended a…

concepts.title: Social Media Perfectionism Girls
concepts.q: How does performative social media amplify anxiety for girls more than playful socializing?
concepts.a: Heavy photo editing and brand-like personas make appearance the prestige game, intensifying comparison, FOMO, and relational aggression. The reward structure itself is the risk.
concepts.definition: Girls' online life skews performative — more time selecting/editing photos, curating image, managing brand-like personas for likes/followers. Prestige domain = appearance + social comparison → social media perfectionism girls amplifies anxiety/depression in girls boys smartphone mental health split (2012 hockey stick). Not playful socializing — "brand managers" per Haidt. FOMO intensifies via Snapchat visibility of excluded gatherings. Relational aggression and trolling layer on performance pressure. haidt four phone norms norm #2 (no social media until 16) targets this directly — no safe minor version when the reward structure is performative prestige.

concepts.title: Socioemotional Selectivity Theory
concepts.q: As perceived time left shrinks, how do goals typically shift according to Carstensen?
concepts.a: From expansive exploration toward emotionally meaningful people and present experiences. Even younger people change choices when time perspective is manipulated.
concepts.definition: Laura Carstensen (Stanford): as perceived time left shrinks, goals shift from expansive exploration toward emotionally meaningful people and experiences. Youth treats "save for retirement / life at 60" as abstract; older adults prioritize family, feelings, and present importance. Lab: manipulating time perspective changes choices even in younger people. alan castel downside in AI era: scams that spoof a loved one's voice exploit this family/emotion focus.

concepts.title: Sodium Intake & Blood Pressure Context
concepts.q: Why is there no universal optimal salt intake recommendation?
concepts.a: Needs depend on blood pressure, activity, stress, and diet. Know your BP status first—low BP/orthostatic issues may need more sodium; hypertension risk rises with high intake on processed-food backgrounds.
concepts.definition: No universal salt recommendation — optimal sodium depends on blood pressure status, activity, stress, and diet. Huberman emphasizes: know your blood pressure (normal, prehypertensive, hypertensive) before changing intake; consult a physician for medical conditions. Population-risk curve (research cited): - ~2 g sodium/day — fewer health risks; risk continues declining toward 4–5 g/day in some analyses. - Higher intake — cardiovascular/stroke risk rises (especially with processed-food background). - ~2.3 g/day — commonly cited low-hazard cutoff in guidelines. Low blood pressure / orthostatic syndromes — dizziness on standing, chronic fatigue, orthostatic hypotension, POTS, idiopathic orthosta…

concepts.title: Soft Tissue Mobilization
concepts.q: What is the real goal of brief foam-rolling or ball work before loading?
concepts.a: A short window of pain reduction and ROM so you can move better—not permanently breaking up fascia. Use it as non-threatening input, then train in the improved range.
concepts.definition: Short (2–3 min) interventions using foam rollers, lacrosse balls, or similar tools to reduce pain and restore range of motion. kelly starrett popularized these tools but emphasizes the goal: window of opportunity — temporary desensitization and ROM gain so you can move better, not permanent structural "breaking up" of fascia. Research-supported effects: pain reduction and ROM restoration (mechanism can stay agnostic). Used by boxers, MMA fighters, and Olympic lifters as non-threatening input before loading. Safe entry point without PT visit — "no one went blind, you didn't dislocate." Pair with breath gated mobility; avoid exceeding breath/voluntary-contraction limits.

concepts.title: Software 3.0
concepts.q: In Karpathy's Software 3.0, what replaces writing explicit code as the main lever?
concepts.a: Prompting and context windows steering an LLM interpreter. Neural nets do more computation; many structured pipelines between input and output become unnecessary.
concepts.definition: Karpathy's framing of computing paradigms: | Era | Programming model | | --- | --- | | Software 1.0 | Explicit code / rules | | Software 2.0 | Data sets + training objectives + neural architectures | | Software 3.0 | Prompting + context window as lever over an LLM interpreter | In Software 3.0, "what text do I copy-paste to my agent?" replaces shell scripts and boilerplate apps. The neural net does more of the computation; structured pipelines between input and output often become unnecessary.

concepts.title: Software Moat Beyond Code
concepts.q: Why wouldn't open-sourcing a mature SaaS codebase let competitors vibe-code a replacement overnight?
concepts.a: Code is only one part: production ops, security, compliance, trust, integrations, and years of edge cases. Complexity and adoption moats remain even if source is free.
concepts.definition: Counter to vibe-coding / AI-apocalypse investor narrative: even if incumbents open-sourced their codebase, competitors would still struggle because writing code is only one part of a software company. The moat includes: - Running production at scale (uptime, patching, dependency vulnerabilities at 3 a.m.) - Security, compliance, enterprise adoption workflows - Customer trust, integrations, and decade of edge-case complexity - Driving adoption and support across organizations amit bendov: CRM and HR are "incredibly complex" — you can't vibe-code a salesforce or gong overnight with 100 engineers; otherwise competitors would already exist. Complements Karpathy's distinction: vibe coding raises …

concepts.title: Software Taste
concepts.q: How is software taste developed rather than treated as an innate gift?
concepts.a: By investing in the last mile beyond requirements—robustness, bonus capability, aesthetics—because you care about solving the problem, not just hitting 100% on the assignment.
concepts.definition: The judgment that separates functional software from exceptional software — not an innate gift but a developed skill built by spending time in the "last mile" beyond requirements. Taste manifests when engineers invest in bonus features, robustness, and expanded capability after hitting 100% on an assignment because they care about solving the problem, not just getting the grade. Karpathy similarly positions taste, aesthetics, and oversight as the human value that survives agentic automation.

concepts.title: Soil Biodiversity Matrix
concepts.q: How does Zach Bush link gut-health talk to soil biodiversity?
concepts.a: Gut discourse describes that soil matters: the gut is the body's soil system. Health is biodiverse cooperation; chemical inputs like glyphosate act as antibiotics demolishing that matrix.
concepts.definition: zach bush: soil is the most complex living system we know — a teaspoon holds more organisms than humans on Earth. Gut health discourse of the last decade is "simply a description that soil is important": the gut is the body's soil system. Health = biodiverse cooperation; demise = monoculture. Chemical inputs (esp. glyphosate) function as antibiotics that demolish that matrix.

concepts.title: Solitude Deprivation
concepts.q: How can you be alone yet still suffer solitude deprivation?
concepts.a: Solitude is absence of stimuli from other minds, not physical isolation. All-day phone input keeps social-processing mode on, exhausting the brain and likely raising anxiety.
concepts.definition: Cal Newport term (Digital Minimalism): solitude = absence of stimuli created by other human minds (not physical isolation). Solitude deprivation = no period in your day free from input originating from other people's brains — all-hands-on-deck social processing (simulating mental states, hierarchy, intent) is cognitively expensive. Pre-smartphone: banishing all solitude was impossible. Smartphones + wireless internet make all-day social-processing mode achievable → hypothesized driver of anxiety rises (brain exhaustion). One of three smartphone harms in Newport's framework (alongside Pavlovian distraction pairing and lost consolidation benefits).

concepts.title: Space-Time Bridging Protocol
concepts.q: How does shifting gaze from eyes-closed body sense to panoramic horizon train goal cognition?
concepts.a: Near/internal focus slices time finely; distant/broad space coarsens time framing for long-range planning. Cycling stations builds flexible switching between interoceptive and exteroceptive modes.
concepts.definition: Huberman's space-time bridging practice trains flexible shifting between peripersonal/interoceptive and extrapersonal/exteroceptive modes — linking visual spatial focus to how the brain batches time for long-range goal cognition. Duration: ~90 s to 3 min (2–3 cycles through stations).

concepts.title: Space-Time-Closeness Attachment Map
concepts.q: In adaptive grief, which attachment dimension should you remap versus keep anchored?
concepts.a: Remap broken space and time predictions (where/when they should be) while anchoring closeness—the bond itself should not be dismantled.
concepts.definition: The brain represents attachments to people, animals, and things along three braided dimensions, not as a simple "emotional closeness" label: | Dimension | What it encodes | Example after loss | | --------- | --------------- | ------------------ | | Space (proximity) | Where they are physically; where belongings/car/bicycle are | Brain still predicts they'll be in familiar locations | | Time (temporal proximity) | When you saw them last, when they'll call, how long to reach them | Predictions fire at habitual contact times | | Closeness (attachment) | Depth of bond; how well you know them | Persists — should not be dismantled in adaptive grief | fMRI experiments (bowling-ball spacing, sound t…

concepts.title: SpaceX Valuation Skepticism
concepts.q: Why might magnificent rocket engineering still not justify SpaceX-as-bubble prospectus claims?
concepts.a: Engineering feats ≠ financial fantasy like asteroid mining or AI-dominated valuation. Starlink can succeed while prospectus promises fail; Grantham would only buy at deep discount.
concepts.definition: jeremy grantham uses SpaceX as bubble-euphoria exemplar — prospectus claims quarter of global GDP as addressable market, asteroid mining, data centers in space; "South Sea bubble" prose parallels. ~90% of theoretical value attributed to AI despite weak frontier-model position. Will fail to deliver prospectus promises (not necessarily company failure — Starlink makes money). Chopstick rocket catch = magnificent engineering but ≠ proof of financial fantasy. Contrast Elon playbook on Tesla: talk stock up 5× fair value → sell → build gigafactory → repeat (self-fulfilling in 6-year bull market). SpaceX scale requires same timing luck unlikely in coming bear phase. Grantham would invest at 5–10 ce…

concepts.title: Speaker Imitation
concepts.q: Why imitate great speakers like a chef copying recipes before innovating?
concepts.a: Copying proven delivery builds skill fastest; then iterate and personalize. Forever copying without adding your flavor fails, but skipping imitation slows mastery.
concepts.definition: The fastest path to becoming a great communicator: deliberately imitate speakers you admire — the same way apprentice chefs copy master recipes before innovating.

concepts.title: Speaking Their Love Language
concepts.q: Why can both partners love authentically yet still feel unloved?
concepts.a: People default to giving in their own love language, but recipients feel care only in theirs. Treat others how they want to be treated, not how you prefer to give.
concepts.definition: The core relational communication insight from five love languages: people show care in their own love language by default, but recipients only feel it when you speak theirs. The Golden Rule revision — "treat others how they want to be treated" — replaces the common misreading of reciprocating your preferences onto others. Symptoms of mismatch: you say "I love you" but they don't feel it; you give time but it's not enough; you do chores but they barely react; gifts don't land; hugs make them pull away. Both parties may be loving authentically while failing to connect. Identification: - Covert — for each person, ask which absence would hurt them most (acknowledgment, time, help, gifts, physic…

concepts.title: Spec + Tickets: Destination vs Journey
concepts.q: What is “Spec + Tickets: Destination vs Journey” and why does it matter?
concepts.a: matt pocock's pattern for spreading agent work across multiple context windows when it won't fit one session: - to-spec compresses the entire grilling discussion (e.g. 46K tokens of Q&A) into a published document — problem statement, solution, user stories, implementation and testing decisions. The spec is the destination: what the codebase looks like at the end. It doubles as the reference the final code review compares against. - to-tickets turns the spec into an implementation plan. Tickets are the journey, and each ticket is deliberately sized to one context window / one smart zone (dumb zone). Ticket bodies stay short because acceptance criteria live in the spec — a ticket is just "what…
concepts.definition: matt pocock's pattern for spreading agent work across multiple context windows when it won't fit one session: - to-spec compresses the entire grilling discussion (e.g. 46K tokens of Q&A) into a published document — problem statement, solution, user stories, implementation and testing decisions. The spec is the destination: what the codebase looks like at the end. It doubles as the reference the final code review compares against. - to-tickets turns the spec into an implementation plan. Tickets are the journey, and each ticket is deliberately sized to one context window / one smart zone (dumb zone). Ticket bodies stay short because acceptance criteria live in the spec — a ticket is just "what…

concepts.title: Specialization After Survival
concepts.q: When should a service business narrow from anyone-with-a-pulse to a focused niche?
concepts.a: After early survival learning—often around $1–3M—narrow to the segment that pays better, is easier to serve, and supports repeatable delivery.
concepts.definition: Early service businesses may accept broad demand to survive and learn, then narrow to the customer segment that pays better, is easier to serve, and enables repeatable delivery — often around $1–3M.

concepts.title: Specific Judgment Fear
concepts.q: What happens when you name the few people behind a vague fear of what people will think?
concepts.a: The avoidance source becomes inspectable and often absurd. Unnamed critics control you; naming them breaks the spell.
concepts.definition: Vague fear of what people will think often derives from the imagined response of a few identifiable people. Naming them makes the source of avoidance inspectable and often absurd.
keywords: Whose Voice Are You Listening To

concepts.title: Specificity in Storytelling
concepts.q: Why do concrete names and sensory details make stories land harder than generic categories?
concepts.a: Listeners see a scene instead of a type—Master Ting vs my teacher. Specificity makes characters human and memorable and surfaces hidden peak moments.
concepts.definition: Add concrete detail — names, physical description, setting, objects — so listeners see a scene instead of a category. Vinh: "When you add more detail, the story comes to life more."

concepts.title: Specs-to-Code Critique
concepts.q: Why is endlessly editing specs instead of shaping AI-generated code still a vibe-coding failure?
concepts.a: Specs are not compiler input; you must understand and battle the code. Alignment and intentional module maps beat treat-the-spec-as-source loops.
concepts.definition: Anti-pattern Matt Pocock tried and rejected: write specifications → pass to AI → if output wrong, edit specs not code → repeat. Essentially vibe coding by another name — ignoring the code battleground. Fails because you must shape and understand code; the spec is not a compiler input. Matt's alternative: Grill Me for alignment → PRD as destination hint (often unread) → issues as journey → intentional module map kept in mind throughout — not spec-to-code.

concepts.title: Speed of Light Engineering
concepts.q: How does comparing designs to physical speed-of-light limits beat continuous 74-to-72-day improvement?
concepts.a: It asks what first-principles rebuild would take against true limits of memory, power, latency, and people—not just incremental shaving of the current plan.
concepts.definition: NVIDIA's 30-year philosophy: compare every design dimension (memory speed, math throughput, power, cost, latency, manufacturing cycle time, headcount) against physical limits ("speed of light") before accepting incremental improvements — strip problems to zero and ask what first-principles rebuild would take six days vs 74.

concepts.title: Sperm Count Decline & Toxicity
concepts.q: What trend makes environmental toxicity a demographic crisis beyond individual infertility?
