concepts.a: Sperm concentration fell from ~100M/mL in 1970 to ~35M/mL today, accelerating ~2.5%/year toward a projected median of zero mid-century if unchecked, with endocrine disruptors and plastics implicated.
concepts.definition: Environmental fertility crisis documented by shanna swan et al. and championed by jeremy grantham via Grantham Foundation: | Era | Sperm concentration (approx.) | |-----|-------------------------------| | Hunter-gatherer | ~118M/mL | | 1970 (first academic reports) | ~100M/mL | | Today | ~35M/mL | | Functional threshold | ~45M/mL (breached ~15–20 yrs ago) | Decline rate accelerating: ~2.5%/year → median male sperm count → zero by ~2045 if unchecked (shanna swan projection). WHO: ~17% of young couples now need fertility help vs ~nil decades ago. Linked drivers: endocrine disruptors fertility (phthalates, BPA, PFAS), microplastics in placenta/breast milk/testicles (100% of testicular tissue sa…

concepts.title: Spiky Founder Pedigree Gap
concepts.q: Why can atypical spiky founders still raise top-tier venture capital?
concepts.a: Investors hire for spikes and visible conviction to build regardless of the check—not polished Stanford-lab generalists. Uneven depth plus drive can beat pedigree.
concepts.definition: Venture-backed success can come from spiky (deeply uneven) founders rather than well-rounded Stanford-PhD-lab-big-co pedigrees. Toby from Shopify frame cited: hire for spikes, not perfect generalists. Conviction + visible drive can attract top-tier capital despite atypical resumes.

concepts.title: Spirulina Rhinitis Protocol
concepts.q: What evidence-backed dose of spirulina is studied for nasal congestion instead of OTC decongestants?
concepts.a: About 2 grams/day in human RCTs reduced obstruction, itching, and inflammatory cytokines and improved sleep and function—without epinephrine-mimetic sleep and dehydration downsides.
concepts.definition: When already symptomatic, spirulina (blue-green algae) offers evidence-backed nasal congestion relief as an alternative to OTC decongestants (Sudafed-class epinephrine mimetics): - Dose: 2 grams/day (not milligrams) - Evidence: Human RCTs, both sexes — 65 and 129 subjects; randomized, double-blind - Outcomes: ↓ nasal obstruction, ↓ nasal itching, improved smell, improved sleep, improved daily function, ↓ inflammatory cytokines OTC decongestant caveats (episode context): epinephrine-system drugs can help symptoms but may dehydrate (hydrate fluids + electrolytes) and disrupt sleep — potentially interfering with underlying healing. Huberman does not prescribe good/bad choice but presents spirul…

concepts.title: Spotify Streaming Commodity
concepts.q: What is lost when streaming turns music into an always-on water faucet?
concepts.a: Album listening rituals, curated physical stashes, and fairer artist pay. Algorithms also pigeonhole genres versus radio-style cross-pollinated discovery.
concepts.definition: Streaming (especially Spotify) turns music into a commodity — "turning on a water faucet" — with massive catalog access and algorithmic discovery, but at the cost of album listening rituals (car drive-through of Led Zeppelin records), physical stash curation, and fair artist compensation. Play counts surface billion-hit signals; podcasts dilute music royalty pools; algorithms can pigeonhole genres vs radio program directors who cross-pollinated discovery (Soundgarden on unexpected rotation).

concepts.title: Spotlight Effect
concepts.q: Why does fear of judgment often overestimate how much others notice your slips?
concepts.a: Everyone feels spotlit while absorbed in their own drama, so few are actually watching you. Realizing how seldom they think of you liberates experimentation.
concepts.definition: A psychological bias: people believe a spotlight is always on them — that others notice and remember every vocal slip, wardrobe choice, or communication experiment. In reality, others are absorbed in their own lives (Mark Twain: you worry less about what people think when you realize how seldom they do). If everyone feels spotlit, nobody is actually watching each other — we are each the main character of our own mental drama.

concepts.title: Stability Fund (Tilbury)
concepts.q: What size and storage rules define Tilbury's stability fund so emergencies don't force bad sales?
concepts.a: About 5× monthly baseline expenses in zero-risk, 24-hour-access high-yield savings earning ~4–5%. Once full, redirect that 15% to growth or rewards.
concepts.definition: The 15% stability bucket in mark tilbury's 25 15 50 10 rule. Target size: 5× monthly baseline — core expenses only (groceries, rent, utilities, transport, work-essential internet). Example: $1,500/mo baseline → $7,500 fund. Purpose: margin for error so one emergency (car breakdown, job loss) doesn't force selling growth investments at a loss or spiraling into debt. Mark's VW Golf engine failure without savings led to cascading loans and a lost year. Storage rules: - Accessible within 24 hours (no lock-up penalties) - Zero risk — never stocks, crypto, or long-term bonds - Must earn 4–5% in high-yield savings (SoFi, Ally, Marcus — FDIC insured) Acceleration tactics: 1. Paycheck sweep — automat…

concepts.title: Stability Passion Relationship Paradox
concepts.q: Why is wanting a safe companion and a risky passionate partner at once so hard?
concepts.a: You cannot fully be both simultaneously; best is oscillating enough novelty (travel, role play, feeling new) to keep desire while preserving long-term security.
concepts.definition: The love-marriage may be a paradox: wanting an emotionally stable, safe companion who is simultaneously a passionate, spontaneous, risky sexual partner — "so difficult if not impossible." You can't fully be both at once; best hope is oscillating proportionally — just enough passion/spontaneity/risk to keep the bedroom alive while maintaining long-term security. Tools without opening the relationship: make partner feel new — different outfits (Victoria's Secret buyers aren't 21-year-olds), role play behind closed doors, travel/foreign bed sheets (context novelty reignites sex for men especially). Steven's 5-year relationship question: how to sustain spontaneity + safety for 50 years — require…

concepts.title: Stablecoin Dollar Access Emerging Markets
concepts.q: How do dollar stablecoins serve people in high-inflation countries better than waiting for local bank fintech?
concepts.a: They provide USD access where banks fail, scam, or charge egregiously and local currencies inflate hundreds of percent yearly—serving ignored geographies first.
concepts.definition: Using stablecoins (especially tether) to give people in inflation/trust-poor countries access to the US dollar — geographies traditional fintech ignores. Banks scam, charge egregious rates, or fail; local currencies inflate hundreds of percent/year (Egypt, Nigeria, Argentina cited).

concepts.title: Stands vs Court
concepts.q: Why is sitting in the stands arguing why it's not your time still an active form of self-sabotage?
concepts.a: You are loudly delaying, comparing, and defeating your own potential—not passively waiting. The alternative is on the court doing imperfect work daily.
concepts.definition: mel robbins binary for life orientation: you are either in the stands (commenting, jealous, delaying, arguing why it's not time) or on the court playing. No middle ground. Being in the stands is loud and active — not passive waiting. Actively arguing against your own potential destroys confidence and possibility; you know it (impostor talk is conscious). Daughter example: years in stands via campaign of misery; work on court = writing crappy songs every day despite comparison evidence around her. Closing charge: get out of the stands and back on the court. Complements Roosevelt/man in the arena (weight critics in the arena) by applying the same geometry to yourself as the sideline critic.

concepts.title: STAR Framework
concepts.q: How does Situation-Task-Action-Result stop rambling under pressure?
concepts.a: It forces thinking before speaking: context, your responsibility, what you did, and the outcome. Each sentence stays intentional instead of thinking aloud.
concepts.definition: A four-step structure for clear communication under pressure — especially behavioral questions, status updates, and accountability conversations. | Step | Meaning | | ---- | ------- | | S — Situation | Set the context briefly | | T — Task | Your responsibility or goal in that context | | A — Action | What you specifically did | | R — Result | Outcome, learning, or next step | Why it works: Most people ramble because they think while speaking. STAR forces you to think before you speak — sharpen thoughts so every sentence is intentional. Demo context (10 communication skills that will make you rich): Peter explains a missed long-form upload — Vinh flew to Malaysia for brother-in-law's wedding;…

concepts.title: Starch as Glucose Education
concepts.q: Why can quitting sugar in tea still leave blood sugar high if bread and cereal remain?
concepts.a: Starch is glucose chains that digestion breaks into blood sugar—holding hands until amylase frees them. Labels' added sugar miss that conversion; total carbs matter.

concepts.title: Stash-Not-Save Mentality
concepts.q: How should teens treat cash differently from saving up to blow on a console?
concepts.a: Stash as a launch pad for making more—keep a seed even after some fun spending. Starting from $0 is hardest; even $100 makes the next money step easier.
concepts.definition: mark tilbury's reframe for teenage cash accumulation: stash money as a launch pad for making more — not "save" in the sense of hoarding then blowing it on a game console or theme park day. The trap: teens save for months, then spend everything on depreciating fun. The fix: ask for cash (not PS4 games) at birthdays/Christmas, take a Saturday job (retail teaches social skills), spend some for fun, but keep a stash that seeds the next money-making step. "The hardest thing in the world is starting with $0 — even $100 makes everything easier." Pairs with age 15 provisional driving license application (UK: 15 years 9 months) so license is ready when needed.

concepts.title: State-Dependent Confidence
concepts.q: Why is calling yourself not a confident person often the wrong diagnosis?
concepts.a: Confidence is usually domain-specific—strong at work yet fragile in romance or speaking. Target the weak cupboard rather than global self-esteem work.
concepts.definition: Confidence is often domain-specific, not a global trait (paul conti). Many people are highly confident in work, athletics, or social settings yet fragile in one area (romance, public speaking, self-image). Labeling yourself "not confident" globally misreads state dependence and skips cupboard-level causes.

concepts.title: Stateless Routine Memory
concepts.q: How should scheduled agents keep learning if every run starts with no chat history?
concepts.a: Persist strategy and lessons in markdown files: read memory, do the job, write updates back. Files are personality and discipline across wake-ups.
concepts.definition: Pattern for scheduled Claude Code agents: each routine run starts stateless with no conversation history. Discipline, strategy, and accumulated learnings live in markdown files on disk (or in a GitHub repo for remote routines). Every run follows read → work → write: 1. Read memory files (CLAUDE.md, strategy, trade log, research log) 2. Execute the routine's job (research, trade, review) 3. Write back lessons and state updates for the next wake-up Treat tokens like money (~200K per routine run); avoid assuming the full 1M context window due to context rot.

concepts.title: Stay Ahead of AI Wrong Question
concepts.q: Why is racing AI on factual recall the wrong human strategy?
concepts.a: It is like Usain Bolt racing a motorcycle—unwinnable and uninteresting. Meaningful human work is judgment, concept creation, and verification, as with physical machines and Olympics.
concepts.definition: Framing error when humans treat frontier LLMs as competitors on factual knowledge or benchmark trivia. ken ono — after struggling to write math problems ChatGPT couldn't solve in the Frontier Math program — concluded "How am I going to stay ahead of AI?" is the wrong question. Competing on information retrieval is like Usain Bolt racing a motorcycle: unwinnable and uninteresting. Society already accepts machines outperforming humans physically (calculators, cars, cranes) while still valuing human Olympic achievement. The parallel shift: brains for deep inquiry now face machines that "caught up" on fact access — but human judgment, concept creation, and verification remain the meaningful aren…
keywords: Stay Ahead of AI Wrong Question

concepts.title: Steroid Merry-Go-Round
concepts.q: Why does casual anabolic use easily become a perpetual merry-go-round?
concepts.a: Gains vanish off-cycle as hormones crash and mood drops, making restarting easy. Long-term health risks mount; informed tradeoffs matter more than instant-gratification culture.
concepts.definition: dorian yates warning on casual anabolic use: - Gains on gear are temporary — muscle lost when you stop; hormones crash → mental depression - Easy to jump back on → perpetual cycle; long-term physical + mental health risks - Many competitors Yates knew are dead from sport-era abuse + diuretics - Women in physique sports now on high doses → mental health toll (under-discussed) - "Instant gratification" culture mirrors cosmetic surgery — ask if lifelong commitment is worth it Not anti-choice — emphasizes informed tradeoffs. Competitive career can justify risk if genetics prove elite early (Yates placed 2nd Night of Champions 1990).

concepts.title: Stick-Not-Carrot Motivation
concepts.q: How does Goggins' stick-and-gas-pedal motivation differ from vision-board carrots?
concepts.a: He does not rely on imagined glory or finish lines—only the stick of outworking the voice that says you will fail. Maybe-maybe is never the fuel.
concepts.definition: david goggins' motivation architecture from how to build immense inner strength david goggins: all stick, no carrot — "stick and gas pedal." He does not visualize paramedic glory, book launches, or finish lines. Even after passing national exams, tomorrow's test still requires 4+ hours of the same page rewrites because retention isn't permanent.

concepts.title: Stimulant Contingency Management
concepts.q: What treatment has the strongest evidence for cocaine/crack when pharmacotherapy has largely failed?
concepts.a: Contingency management: escalating cash rewards for negative urine screens. It shows control is impaired but not zero, and is now often insurance-covered.
concepts.definition: Biggest disappointment in addiction field: ~40 years, almost no progress on crack/cocaine pharmacotherapy. Contingency management (Steve Higgins) = only strong evidence: escalating cash rewards for negative urine screens ($2 → $4 → $8 → $16…). Proves addicts have impaired but not zero control. Now insurance-covered in many places. Prescription stimulants (Adderall/Vyvanse): transformative for some ADHD kids, overprescription worry, medicalizing normal variation; untreated ADHD may raise later substance-abuse risk (debated).

concepts.title: Stimulate, Recover, Adapt
concepts.q: Why aren't you growing muscle during the workout itself in Yates' training loop?
concepts.a: Training stimulates damage/stress; growth is adaptation after recovery. Hitting the wall again mid-rebuild wastes the cycle—failure modes are too little stimulus or too little recovery.
concepts.definition: dorian yates's core training loop (preferred title over "Pumping Iron"): 1. Stimulate — Overload muscle beyond status quo (muscular failure training); you are not growing during the workout — creating damage/stress 2. Recover — Rest, nutrition, sleep before returning; knocking the wall down again mid-rebuild wastes the cycle ("rehabbing your house" analogy) 3. Adapt — Overcompensation after recovery → larger/stronger tissue Failure modes: insufficient intensity (no stimulus) OR excessive volume/frequency (can't recover). Natural trainees need longer between sessions than enhanced athletes. Pairs training deload six two cycle when progress stalls ~6 weeks.

concepts.title: Stop Condition (Agent Loops)
concepts.q: What makes a valid stop condition for an agentic loop?
concepts.a: An externally verifiable completion check against real system state (no open high-priority errors, deployed improvements), not the agent declaring itself done.
concepts.definition: The objective completion criterion that terminates an agentic loops. Must be externally verifiable — the agent checks real system state, not self-declared "done."

concepts.title: Story Bank
concepts.q: Why document ordinary events as story seeds before you need them socially?
concepts.a: Sharing stories builds rapport fast, but only if you captured them. Prepared narrative beats improvised panic and proves you do have things worth saying.
concepts.definition: A personal habit of documenting things that happen to you so you always have stories ready to share in conversation. Vinh Giang calls sharing stories one of the fastest ways to build rapport and self-confidence — but stories only exist if you capture them. Not the same as a polished origin story interview (2–3 min hiring narrative), but the same underlying asset: prepared narrative beats improvised panic.

concepts.title: Story Symbolize Link
concepts.q: After telling a personal anecdote to a client, what must you do so it does not feel irrelevant?
concepts.a: Ask what the story symbolizes, bridge that symbol to their problem, and say the reason I'm telling you this is… Explicit linkage creates rapport before advice.
concepts.definition: While listening to a personal story, ask "What does this story symbolize?" — then explicitly bridge that symbol to the listener's situation before delivering advice or service. Vinh Giang uses this in client-facing contexts (e.g. a physical therapist opening with a New Zealand trip story): hunger pains and sore feet symbolize unaddressed physical pain → treating loved ones badly when you're not at your best → why fixing the client's back pain matters for their whole life. Close with a direct link phrase: "The reason I'm telling you this story is…" so the listener never wonders why the anecdote was relevant.

concepts.title: Strategic Partnerships (1+1=11)
concepts.q: How can a partnership make 1+1=11 better than buying more ads?
concepts.a: Find companies whose audience already matches your demographic and propose win-win revenue share. Mutual lists grow both sides without Facebook spend.
concepts.definition: simon squibb's label for partnerships where 1 + 1 = 11: find companies whose email/database already contains your exact demographic, propose win-win revenue share + mission alignment, instead of spending on Facebook ads.

concepts.title: Strategic vs Tactical Programming
concepts.q: In the AI era, where does human leverage shift between tactical and strategic programming?
concepts.a: Models cheaply handle day-to-day code and fixes. Humans must own long-term architecture, interfaces, tests, and docs that let agents change systems safely.
concepts.definition: Distinction from John Ousterhout's A Philosophy of Software Design applied to the AI era: - Tactical programming — day-to-day code writing, syntax, bug fixes, commits. - Strategic programming — long-term codebase shape, velocity strategies, module interfaces, test strategy, documentation that guides change. AI has effectively eliminated the need for humans to excel at tactical programming — models do it cheaper. Human leverage shifts entirely to strategic programming: designing systems agents can change safely and delegating well-scoped tactical work.

concepts.title: Stress-Recovery Capacity Balance
concepts.q: What happens when visible plus hidden stress exceeds recovery capacity?
concepts.a: You regress or adapt less. Maximize productive stress then recover fully—or cut load / raise capacity—using gauges like morning grip strength drops.
concepts.definition: andy galpin's recovery framework (Biomolecular Athlete program): Three buckets: visible stressors, hidden stressors, and recovery capacity. When total stress load exceeds recovery capacity → regression or reduced adaptability. Goal: maximize implementable stress (driver of adaptation), then recover fully. Practical gauges (anecdata + emerging research): - Grip strength on waking — squeeze grip tool or floor scale; ≥10–20% force drop vs well-rested baseline → nerve-to-muscle pathways still remodeling (systemic, not just trained muscle) - carbon dioxide discard rate — morning CO2 blow-off after 4+1 breaths; <25–30 s = under-recovered, 30–60 s = green, 65–120 s = nervous system ready; Galpin la…

concepts.title: Stress Response Energy Theft
concepts.q: Why does rumination steal more vitality than the brief stressor that triggered it?
concepts.a: Acute spikes pass; chronic worry mounts a sustained metabolic response that diverts energy from growth, maintenance, and repair. Awareness lets you choose not to mount the full response.
concepts.definition: It's not stress that burns us down — it's the response to stress. Acute stressors (bad email) spike briefly; rumination about past/future and chronic worry mount a sustained metabolic response that steals energy from growth, maintenance, and repair in the finite energy budget.

concepts.title: Stress & Sodium Craving
concepts.q: Why might salt craving under stress be an evolved feature rather than a pathology?
concepts.a: Adrenal stress hormones co-regulate fluid and salt appetite because challenges need vascular volume and neural function. Too little sodium impairs meeting stress; modest increases may help some people.
concepts.definition: The stress system (adrenal glucocorticoids including aldosterone) is hardwired to regulate fluid balance and salt appetite because stress challenges (infection, famine, dehydration, physical threat) share a generic response: elevated heart rate, elevated blood pressure, mobilization. Too little sodium → impaired ability to meet stress challenges. Under stress, a natural craving for sodium emerges — not pathology but an evolved mechanism to support vascular volume and neural function during challenge. Some individuals report that modest sodium increase (via healthy unprocessed sources) may reduce anxiety — explore individually with medical oversight. Kidneys + adrenal glands co-regulate salt/…

concepts.title: Stress Three Time Scales
concepts.q: How should stress tools differ for acute minutes versus chronic months?
concepts.a: Acute stress can sharpen focus—use sighs and movement. Medium term builds threshold under load. Chronic (marked by broken sleep) must be turned off via sleep, exercise, connection, and recovery.
concepts.definition: Huberman organizes stress interventions by three time scales, each with different biology and tools: | Scale | Duration | Role | Key tools | |-------|----------|------|-----------| | Short-term (acute) | Moments to hours | Mobilization; often beneficial — sharpens focus, primes immune system | physiological sigh; accept/agitate-through movement | | Medium-term | Days to ~2 months | Stress threshold building — capacity to stay cognitively calm while body is activated | stress threshold panoramic gaze; cold/sprint + calm mind; cyclic hyperventilation when run-down | | Long-term (chronic) | Months+ when sleep fails | Harmful — heart disease, withered hippocampus imagery; must turn stress respon…

concepts.title: Stress Threshold & Panoramic Gaze
concepts.q: How does widening your gaze while heart rate is high raise stress threshold?
concepts.a: Stress narrows vision into tunnel mode tied to autonomic arousal. Deliberately panoramic gaze calms the mind while the body stays activated, training calm under physiological load.
concepts.definition: Stress threshold = cognitive capacity to regulate the body's stress state — how much activation you can tolerate while staying mentally calm. Built over medium-term (days to ~2 months), not years. Training protocol: Deliberately raise bodily activation (cold shower, ice bath, cyclic hyperventilation, sprint, hard bike interval — heart at ~80–90% max), then calm the mind while the body stays activated. Goal: dissociate mental/emotional panic from high physiological output (not clinical dissociation). Key tool — panoramic gaze: Stress dilates pupils → tunnel vision (literally narrows visual field). The visual system is tethered to the autonomic nervous system via cranial nerves. Deliberately w…

concepts.title: Structure of Self
concepts.q: Why assess psyche structure before chasing behavioral confidence hacks?
concepts.a: Symptoms often reflect architecture—unconscious mind, defenses, character patterns, self-concept—like organs in medicine. Structural issues are not moral failures fixed by tips alone.
concepts.definition: The architecture of psyche (paul conti): (1) unconscious conscious iceberg, (2) defense mechanisms, (3) character structure (predispositions + habitual action patterns), and (4) self-concept / sense of self. Parallels physical medicine's "structure" — bones, organs, systems.

concepts.title: Student Debt Professional Trap
concepts.q: How can education debt turn a career misfit into psychological purgatory?
concepts.a: Huge undergrad plus professional loans lock you in even after discovering you hate the work. Identity becomes I work to pay bills—debt owns options more than passion does.
concepts.definition: Career lock-in from compounded US education debt: ~$150,000 undergraduate loans plus ~$200,000 professional school — then discovering you "can't stand the sight of blood" (or equivalent misfit) but cannot leave because loan payments own your options. ken ono calls this purgatory: life reduces to "I go to work because it pays the bills" — identity surrendered to debt servitude rather than passion. Contrasts wonder driven learning and choosing fields from curiosity. Pairs with ben felix rich is simple doac human-capital framing and honest advice financial freedom gabe bult freedom-number alternative — but Ono emphasizes the psychological trap of wrong-profession debt, not just financial math.

concepts.title: Study Schedule Entrainment
concepts.q: Why does studying at the same times for a few days improve focus at those hours?
concepts.a: The brain entrains like sleep-wake cycles; after ~3 days, regularity alone lowers the cost of attending then. You still pull focus back, but scheduled slots help.
concepts.definition: The brain entrains to regular study times similarly to sleep-wake cycles — after ~3 days of studying at consistent times (1–2 blocks/day, e.g. early + late), focus and attention improve at those times by virtue of regularity alone. Mechanism: exposure to sunlight, activities, and social rhythms train active/inactive periods. Focus during study is not automatic — you must repeatedly pull attention back to the page or lecture — but scheduled times reduce the cost. Protocol: Pick regular study slot(s) → allow 2–3 day transition → maintain schedule (including weekends near exam periods if needed). Phone off, notify others, limit Wi-Fi. Focus-as-study is a learnable skill accelerated by entrainme…

concepts.title: Sub-Agents and Agent Teams
concepts.q: When are sub-agents valuable, and what cache cost should you budget for?
concepts.a: They excel at parallel specialized tasks with clean context; teams coordinate separate workstreams. Sub-agents often use a short ~5-minute prompt-cache TTL, so many parallel runs recache more often.
concepts.definition: Advanced Claude Code patterns for delegating work. Sub-agents handle specialized subtasks with focused context. Agent teams coordinate parallel agents on separate branches or workstreams. Some features (e.g. /agents command) are CLI-only and may not appear in IDE extension or desktop app surfaces. Cache note: sub-agents use 5-minute prompt caching TTL regardless of subscription plan — more frequent recache than main Claude Code sessions (1 hour). Factor into token economics when running many parallel sub-agents.

concepts.title: Subflow Timeout Callback
concepts.q: How do you deliver long-running n8n sub-workflow results when the parent agent times out waiting?
concepts.a: Have the sub-workflow send the final output itself (Telegram, Sheets) instead of returning through the agent path. The parent may show failure while the user still gets the result.
concepts.definition: A workaround for n8n's behavior when an AI agent waits on a sub-workflow tool that contains long Wait steps (e.g. polling image/video generation). The agent-level call times out and errors even though the sub-workflow eventually succeeds. The fix: have the sub-workflow deliver the final result directly (e.g. send video to Telegram, log to Google Sheets) instead of returning through the agent's response path. The main agent run may show a failed Telegram node; the user still receives the output from the sub-workflow's own delivery nodes. Nate notes n8n waits at the agent level rather than the tool level — likely to improve as the platform evolves.

concepts.title: Subjective Age Longevity
concepts.q: Why might how old you feel predict longevity better than calendar age?
concepts.a: After ~40 many feel ~20% younger; that subjective age forecasts lifespan better than biological age alone. Over-identifying with chronological scripts can cue stereotype-congruent decline.
concepts.definition: Subjective age = how old you feel (physical + mental), distinct from chronological/biological age. alan castel: after ~40, most people feel about 20% younger than calendar age. Critically, subjective age is a better predictor of longevity than biological age — yet doctors ask chronological age, which can cue stereotype-congruent behavior ("I should act like a 75-year-old"). Felt youth can come from coaching kids, campus life, injury contrast days — not marathon performance. Don't over-subscribe to age scripts.

concepts.title: Sugar Addiction Brain Circuits
concepts.q: How does non-fruit fructose create addiction-like brain changes beyond preference?
concepts.a: It downregulates dopamine receptors like drugs of abuse and, with insulin blocking leptin, leaves the brain deaf to satiety so craving circuits remap toward more sugar.
concepts.definition: Non-fruit fructose activates brain reward pathways similarly to drugs of abuse — downregulating dopamine receptors and altering craving/satisfaction circuits. robert lustig frames this as addiction, not mere preference.

concepts.title: Sun Gulpa Personal Compass
concepts.q: Why phrase a Yoga Nidra sankalpa as I deserve to be whole rather than I am whole after trauma?
concepts.a: It is an aspirational compass for belief change, not a premature affirmation that denies reality. Directional intention guides identity shift before behavior follows.
concepts.definition: Sankalpa (intention/mantra) used as life compass in Yoga Nidra — e.g. trauma patient: "I deserve to be whole" (aspirational) not "I am whole" (premature affirmation).

concepts.title: Superpowers Skill
concepts.q: What senior-developer workflow does the Superpowers Claude plugin force before coding?
concepts.a: Plan first, isolate environment, write tests before implementation, brainstorm, then two-stage review for spec and quality—raising first-pass completeness and cutting rework tokens.
concepts.definition: Community Claude Code plugin (superpowers@claude-plugins-official) that forces a senior-developer workflow: plan before code, work in an isolated environment, write tests before implementation, brainstorm, then two-stage self-review (spec compliance, then code quality). Targets rushed one-shot code that looks fine until run in production. Realistic expectation: ~80% complete on first pass vs ~60% without structured QA — fewer debug cycles and lower token spend on rework. 150K+ GitHub stars cited.

concepts.title: Supplier Financing (No Capital)
concepts.q: If you lack cash for first inventory, what can you ask suppliers instead of waiting to save $4,000?
concepts.a: Ship now and pay after sales at a premium, or take equity. If you don't ask you don't get—many say yes to clear concepts; worst case is no.
concepts.definition: When you lack ~$4,000 for first inventory, ask suppliers to back you: ship goods now, pay after sales at a premium, or take an equity stake. Airbnb sold cereal boxes and rented spare rooms to fund tech — same inventiveness applies to physical product.

concepts.title: Synthesis Skill on Novel Problems
concepts.q: What skill does Po-Shen Loh test by giving hints on a problem a student has never seen?
concepts.a: Synthesis under novelty—combining partial hints into a coherent solution—not recall or AI delegation. Speed of flexible thinking under genuine surprise is the signal.
concepts.definition: The ability to combine partial hints into a coherent solution for a problem never encountered before — distinct from recalling a memorized method or delegating to AI. po shen loh operationalizes this in high-school interviews: ask questions until body language clearly shows the student has never seen this problem, then give hints and measure how quickly they synthesize into a solution. Expectation: they won't solve it alone; the signal is synthesis speed under novelty. This is "creativity" in the useful sense — not decorative originality but mental flexibility under genuine surprise. Pairs with creative thinking novel problems and contrasts test prep cramming kills invention (pre-exposure re…

concepts.title: System Evolution
concepts.q: After fixing an agent bug or failure, what should you do beyond the one-off repair?
concepts.a: Upgrade the agent system so the failure cannot recur: new CLAUDE.md rules, planning docs, skill updates, or hooks. Treat every bug as a permanent upgrade opportunity, not just a patch.
concepts.definition: The fourth step of plan build verify evolve: after fixing a bug or failure, upgrade the agent system so it cannot happen again. Treat Claude Code like an employee or co-founder that learns over time. Upgrade targets: - New rules in CLAUDE.md - Planning documents referenced at task start - Skill updates (validation steps, guardrails) - Hooks that enforce checks automatically Mindset shift: every bug is a permanent upgrade — welcome failures as data; nervous when everything goes too well because there's nothing to improve. Nate's accidental mass email → team case study → agent rule update is the canonical ops example. Proactive edge cases: ask "how could this go wrong?" before failures; code r…

concepts.title: Low-Dose Tadalafil for Men's Health
concepts.q: Why might a clinician prescribe 2.5-5 mg tadalafil daily for reasons other than ED?
concepts.a: Low-dose daily tadalafil can support prostate health and genital blood flow, cut nocturia roughly in half (improving sleep), mildly lower blood pressure, and may increase androgen receptor density. Keep routine doses at or below 10 mg/day under medical supervision.
concepts.definition: Tadalafil (Cialis) at 2.5–5 mg/day — prescribed off-label for prostate health and blood flow, not erectile dysfunction treatment per se. Gillett: "very underrated medication." Benefits: - Prostate health / genital blood flow support - Nocturia — cuts nighttime urination episodes ~in half (e.g., 2→1), improving sleep → secondary GH/testosterone optimization - Mild blood pressure reduction (also a BP medication class) - Androgen receptor density increase — same mechanism as l carnitine androgen receptor density Dosing guardrails: - Typical prostate protocol: 2.5–5 mg/day - Do not exceed 10 mg/day routinely - 20 mg = high dose; can affect red-green cone discrimination (reversible on cessation) …

concepts.title: Talent Tree Framework
concepts.q: How does the talent-tree approach differ from school-style weakness-fixing for career focus?
concepts.a: Identify your natural category—Organizer, Analyst, Communicator, or Creative—and work where you have clear advantage. Commit to talent-maxxing instead of spreading effort fixing every weakness.
concepts.definition: mark tilbury's talent-maxxing model: identify your natural talent category and commit to working only where you have a clear advantage — opposite of the school system's weakness-fixing approach. Four categories on the "talent tree": | Type | Strength | | --- | --- | | Organizer | Structure, planning, systems, keeping things running | | Analyst | Breaking things down, spotting patterns, figuring what works | | Communicator | Explaining clearly, influencing, getting buy-in | | Creative | Novel connections, seeing differently | Evidence cited: 98% of five-year-olds score genius-level on divergent thinking; by adulthood only 2% — talent is trained out, not absent.

concepts.title: Tallest Tree Entrepreneur Culture
concepts.q: In Chinese capitalist culture, what unwritten rule limits how successful founders should behave publicly?
concepts.a: Success is encouraged, but political visibility and influence are not. Capital must stay subordinate to the political class; standing out on politics invites scrutiny, while humility and collaboration are safer.
concepts.definition: Unwritten rule for Chinese capitalists: success is encouraged; political visibility and influence are not. Capital must remain subordinate to the political class. Standing out publicly — especially on politics — invites scrutiny; humility, philanthropy, and government collaboration are safer.

concepts.title: Task Bracketing
concepts.q: What does the brain learn about a habit beyond the individual steps of the behavior itself?
concepts.a: Via the dorsolateral striatum, it learns the start/stop envelope—what precedes and follows execution—so the whole sequence feels necessary and reflexive, like brushing teeth even after bad sleep.
concepts.definition: Neural start/stop framing around a behavior, mediated by the dorsolateral striatum (basal ganglia). The brain learns not just the habit steps but the envelope — what precedes and follows execution — making the whole sequence feel necessary and reflexive (like brushing teeth even after bad sleep).

concepts.title: Task Difficulty Agent Scaling
concepts.q: How should agent count, planning depth, and model choice change as a coding task goes from trivial to extreme?
concepts.a: Match them to difficulty (1-10): simple UI tweaks need one agent and no plan mode; mid complexity adds plan mode; hardest infra work may need a lead plus several sub-agents. Avoid over-engineering easy tasks.
concepts.definition: Framework for matching agent count, planning depth, and model choice to task complexity on a 1–10 scale. As difficulty rises, add agents, enable plan mode, and sometimes launch sub-agents — but don't over-engineer simple tasks. | Difficulty | Example | Agents | Plan | Notes | |------------|---------|--------|------|-------| | 1 | Button color, copy change | 1 | No | Voice prompt + @ directory | | 2 | Sign-up UI page (design only) | 1 | No | Claude Opus for UI | | 5–6 | NestJS API + Drizzle + Cognito + Discord | 1 | Yes | GPT-5.5 Extra High Fast for backend | | 10 | Full AWS VPC/ECR/ECS/RDS | 1 lead + 5 sub-agents | Yes | Claude Code Extra High; YOLO mode; 30–60 min |

concepts.title: Task Switching Cost Cognition
concepts.q: Why is frequently shifting attention between tasks more costly than the minutes spent on each switch?
concepts.a: Shifting focus is an expensive neuronal operation that leaves energy drain and cognitive residue, degrading later deep work. Phones excel at inducing these switches via notifications and in-app hopping.
concepts.definition: The expensive neuronal operation of shifting focus of attention from one task/network to another — cannot happen in two seconds. Smartphones excel at inducing these switches (in-app context hopping + notification pulls). Cost is not just time spent on phone but energy and cognitive residue degrading subsequent deep work. Cal Newport: goes 2–4 h without phone → "text bankruptcy" on accumulated messages. Huberman junior-professor hack: give phone to lab member with cash penalty if retrieved before 5 PM. Complements Huberman smartphone attention erosion daily limits — same mechanism, Newport emphasizes network-switching physics.

concepts.title: Task Switching Transition Gaps
concepts.q: What simple design between tasks helps the prefrontal cortex switch more cleanly than willpower or stimulants?
concepts.a: Insert designated, information-light pauses so circuits from task A can disengage before task B starts. Huberman frames these transition gaps as an underused, evidence-supported lever.
concepts.definition: Designated, information-light pauses between cognitive or physical tasks that let the prefrontal cortex disengage circuits from task A before engaging task B. Huberman frames this as the most under-discussed, evidence-supported lever for better task switching — not willpower, not stimulants.

concepts.title: Tax-Advantaged Investing Accounts
concepts.q: Why do governments limit contributions to accounts like Roth IRAs, ISAs, or TFSAs?
concepts.a: They let investments grow without taxing gains, which is powerful for compounding. Contribution caps exist because that tax shelter is so advantageous; opening the right account early matters more than fancy stock picks.
concepts.definition: Government-sanctioned wrappers that let investments grow without paying taxes on gains — but with contribution limits because they are powerful. | Region | Account | Benefit | | --- | --- | --- | | USA | Roth IRA | Post-tax in; tax-free growth and retirement withdrawals; $7K/yr (<50); Peter Thiel $5B example | | USA | 401(k) | Pre-tax paycheck contributions; employer match = free money | | UK | Stocks & shares ISA | Up to £20K/yr; all gains tax-free | | UK | Workplace pension | Typically 5% employee + 3% employer match | | Canada | TFSA | Tax-free growth | | Australia | Superannuation | Retirement tax advantages | Opening the correct account type at 18 is step three of teen financial roadmap…

concepts.title: Teach Skill
concepts.q: How does the stateful Teach skill structure learning differently from dumping a topic into chat?
concepts.a: It starts from the user's mission, clarifies goals into mission.md, builds cheat sheets and HTML lessons, quizzes for storage strength, and tracks a learning record locally so courses persist across sessions.
concepts.definition: Stateful procedure skill from matt pocock's skills repo that generates personalized courses on any topic. Encodes teaching principles: zone of proximal development, knowledge/skills/wisdom distinction, mission alignment before content. Workflow: 1. User states mission (what they want to achieve), not just subject. 2. Skill asks clarifying questions; writes mission.md. 3. Researches trusted resources; creates reference cheat sheet + HTML lessons (richer than terminal). 4. Tracks learning record; quizzes for storage strength; linear path through knowledge graph. 5. Invites follow-up questions and next lesson generation. Install: npx skills add mattpocock/skills → select teach. Works in Claude …

concepts.title: Teach to Learn
concepts.q: Why do top students often benefit from teaching peers even if they fear giving classmates a freebie?
concepts.a: Explaining material forces testing and mastery. Teachers clearly gain from the retrieval and gap-exposure of teaching, regardless of whether peers also benefit competitively.
concepts.definition: Study protocol where students learn material in isolation, then teach peers in the same course. Top-performing medical students in the ~700-student survey "perform exceedingly well" vs classmates. Competitive fear reframe: Students worry teaching gives classmates a "freebie" or creates competitive disadvantage. The survey didn't analyze whether learners got unfair advantage — but teachers clearly benefit from the testing/mastery function of explaining material. Pairs with watch one do one teach one lab tradition: teaching is the final stage that exposes gaps and solidifies understanding.

concepts.title: Team of Rivals Brain
concepts.q: If humans are not a single unified self, how should we think about conflicting desires like eating cookies vs staying lean?
concepts.a: Competing neural networks form a neural parliament whose majority vote drives behavior. Different coalitions win in different contexts, so know thyselves (plural) and which networks activate when.
concepts.definition: david eagleman dreams brain plasticity doac reframe: humans are not a single indivisible self but competing neural networks with different drives — a "neural parliament" whose majority vote determines behavior moment to moment. Example: chocolate cookies → eat-for-energy network vs weight-gain network vs "one cookie + gym tonight" network. Retrospective regret ("why did I eat the whole bag?") = a different coalition was in charge under different circumstances. Implication: know thyselves (plural), not "know thyself." Behavior prediction requires understanding which networks activate in which contexts. jordan peterson + andrew huberman parallel: hypothalamus (primitive drives — rage, reproduc…

concepts.title: Teaspoons of Sugar Equivalent
concepts.q: How can a GP make the glucose impact of starchy foods intuitive in a short consultation?
concepts.a: Convert glycemic load into 4-gram teaspoon-of-sugar cubes for a standard portion, then compare foods side by side. Starch becomes glucose too, so cereal, potato, or rice can equal many teaspoons despite little labeled sugar.

concepts.title: Technical Interviews Holistic AI Era
concepts.q: When AI can answer any interview question, how should hiring filters change?
concepts.a: Drop questions AI already aces. Interviews become holistic: culture fit, past work discussion, and short rapport—because AI already knows skills and build history, enabling matching without long assessment gauntlets.
concepts.definition: When AI can answer any interview question, filter those questions out. roy lee predicts interviews become holistic: culture fit, past work discussion, 30-minute rapport — because AI already knows everything you've built and your skill profile. Direct job matching possible without one-hour assessment gauntlets. Applies beyond software to all fields. Current Cluely hiring: mostly conversation + culture fit + past work review.

concepts.title: Technology Progress Not Guaranteed
concepts.q: Why is it wrong to assume technological progress will automatically keep advancing forever?
concepts.a: Civilizations can lose capabilities (pyramids, aqueducts, dark ages). Progress continues only because passionate people work extremely hard; the last century's gains are not assured.
concepts.definition: Technological advancement is not automatic — civilizations can lose capabilities (Egyptian pyramids, Roman aqueducts, dark ages). The last hundred years of positive progress are not guaranteed to continue; technology advances because passionate people work extremely hard.

concepts.title: Technology Readiness Rate of Change
concepts.q: What two signals suggest a technology is ready for broader product adoption?
concepts.a: Early adopters are already doing interesting things with it, and underlying capability shows a visible rate of change—like successive phone camera jumps or model leaps from Claude 3 to 3.5.
concepts.definition: Product timing heuristic from mike krieger: find technologies ready for broader adoption than they currently have, signaled by (1) early adopters already doing interesting things in the space, and (2) visible rate of change in underlying capability — e.g. iPhone 3→3GS→4 camera/network jumps, or Claude 3→3.5 leaps.

concepts.title: Teen Financial Roadmap (Tilbury)
concepts.q: What is the core thesis of Tilbury's age-by-age teen money checklist from 13 to 18+?
concepts.a: Time in the market is the biggest investing advantage; skipping earlier steps compounds regret later. Progress through custodial investing, talent exploration, stashing income, skill stacking, and correct adult accounts.
concepts.definition: mark tilbury's age-by-age checklist for getting financially ahead from 13 through 18 — and a seven-step adult checklist at 18+. Thesis: time in the market is the single biggest investing advantage; skipping earlier steps compounds regret later. Watch all ages, not just your current one. | Age | Focus | | --- | --- | | 13 | Open custodial investing account with parent/guardian; start S&P 500 index fund habit | | 14 | Explore talents freely (no rent pressure); discipline from non-money activities (son Curtis: 4 AM swimming) | | 15 | stash not save mentality — cash gifts + Saturday job; apply for provisional driving license (UK: 15y9m) | | 16 | Stack skills on equipment investment (son's $500 i…

concepts.title: Teflon Buddha Criticism Defense
concepts.q: How can someone become psychologically less sticky to criticism without empty self-praise?
concepts.a: Build Teflon-Buddha imperviousness by understanding poison-dart criticism: attacks hit linguistic weak points, not volume. Narcissistic I'm-great deflection fails when identity is still brittle.
concepts.definition: Build psychological imperviousness to criticism — like a "Teflon Buddha" — by understanding poison dart criticism precision: attacks target linguistic weak points, not volume; narcissists deflect with "I'm great" until identity shifts.

concepts.title: Telegram Lean Engineering
concepts.q: How can roughly 40 engineers serve a billion users, and what does that imply about large teams?
concepts.a: Telegram runs lean: constraint forces automation (algorithms manage about 100K servers). Employee quantity often inversely correlates with quality—coordination overhead and idle people invent problems that demotivate A-players.
concepts.definition: Telegram's operating model: ~40 core engineers (backend, frontend, designers, sysadmins) serving 1B+ users on ~100K automated servers. Philosophy: employee quantity often inversely correlates with product quality — coordination overhead consumes 90% of large teams; idle employees demotivate A-players and invent problems. Constraint forces automation: algorithms manage global infrastructure instead of thousands of manual operators.

concepts.title: Telegram Speed Engineering
concepts.q: Why does Telegram treat even about 50ms delays as a first-class product problem?
concepts.a: Users feel tiny delays; faster code needs fewer servers and costs less. At billion-user scale, half-second delays waste centuries of collective time, so they obsess over weak-device performance.
concepts.definition: Speed as core product philosophy inherited from VK: users subconsciously feel even ~50ms delays; faster code = fewer servers = lower cost. At 1B users opening dozens of times daily, half-second delays waste "centuries" of collective human time. VK beat Facebook load times in 2009 Silicon Valley demos via custom C/C++ data engines (Nikolai Durov). Telegram obsesses over weak-device performance (animated gradients, particle delete effects on oldest phones).

concepts.title: Temperature Minimum
concepts.q: How can you estimate when your core body temperature hits its daily low without measuring temperature?
concepts.a: Your temperature minimum is about two hours before your average wake time. Log wake time daily to refine the estimate.
concepts.definition: Your temperature minimum is the time of day when core body temperature reaches its lowest point in the 24-hour cycle. Huberman estimates it as approximately 2 hours before your average wake time. Log wake time daily to refine the estimate — you do not need to measure actual temperature.

concepts.title: Ten Cupboards Exercise
concepts.q: When hunting the root of anxiety or a confidence gap, why check many psychological cupboards before concluding?
concepts.a: Answers may live in unconscious content, defenses, character, drives, biology, relationships, trauma, and more. Look in all of them before medicating or locking onto one explanation.
concepts.definition: paul conti's spirited inquiry method: when seeking the root of a mental-health pattern (anxiety, confidence gap, narrative), assume answers may live in multiple psychological "cupboards" — unconscious content, defenses, character structure, drives, biology, relationships, trauma history, etc. Look in all of them before concluding or medicating.

concepts.title: Ten Financial Mistakes (Ben Felix)
concepts.q: Beyond stock-picking, what kinds of quiet errors most damage long-term wealth according to Ben Felix?
concepts.a: Not earning or saving enough, unclear life goals, overspending on the wrong things, avoiding productive investment risk, taking bad speculative risks, and similar checklist failures that compound over decades.
concepts.definition: Checklist from ben felix (ben felix rich is simple doac) — common errors that quietly damage long-term wealth: 1. Not earning enough — treat income as fixable via human capital (education, skills, entrepreneurship); rare complementary skill stacks + right market/industry (biotech writer 5× generic writer pay) 2. Not saving enough — compounding missed; wake-up at 50–60 too late; contrasts with young-people over-saving pressure (lifecycle research) 3. (Goals) — lacking clear life vision — see perma financial goals 4. Overspending on wrong things — spending that doesn't serve desired life blocks saving for what does 5. Not taking investment risk — skipping/under-weighting stocks forfeits ~7% ex…

concepts.title: Test Prep Cramming Kills Invention
concepts.q: How can intensive test-prep drilling hurt students beyond overload and burnout?
concepts.a: By pre-exposing every strange question type, it removes the chance to invent under novelty. Parents hope for zero surprise on test day, but that kills creative problem-solving that unseen questions once trained.
concepts.definition: Large industry around math competition and standardized test preparation that pre-exposes students to every possible strange question through many hours of drilling — so parents hope test day brings zero surprise. po shen loh argues this is bad for students (school + after-school overload) and worse because it removes the student's chance to invent. Contrasts with creative thinking novel problems ethos where unseen questions were the training ground. Symptom parallel to ai cheating incentive misalignment and education checkbox gpa system.

concepts.title: Testing Effect Learning
concepts.q: Why are quizzes powerful even when they are not used for grading?
concepts.a: Retrieval practice is one of the strongest learning tools: testing builds knowledge, improves retention, and offsets forgetting. Active recall during learning differs from post-session biology like sleep consolidation.
concepts.definition: Tests and quizzes are not only evaluation — they are among the strongest learning tools in peer-reviewed literature. Retrieval practice builds knowledge, improves retention, and offsets forgetting. Huberman demonstrates meta-pedagogy in the clip: after teaching study habits, he runs a pop quiz ("name three tools…") — the quiz itself is the point. Effective-student tools recalled: phone away + notify others, study alone, teach peers. Distinct from understand improve memory science based tools huberman essentials encoding tools (adrenaline spike, sleep consolidation) — this is active recall during learning, not post-session biology. alan castel: use names several times (deliberate/retrieval pr…

concepts.title: Testosterone Decline in Modern Men
concepts.q: What has happened to average male testosterone over recent decades, and what drives it more than aging alone?
concepts.a: Averages fell roughly 600 to 450 ng/dL (about 25%) from late 1990s to 2015. Drivers include endocrine disruptors, obesity/aromatase, diabetes, ultraprocessed food, stress, and poor sleep; symptoms matter more than a single number.
concepts.definition: Population-level androgen drop driven by lifestyle + environment: - Average total T: ~600 ng/dL (late 1990s) → ~450 (2015) — ~25% decline - Drivers: endocrine disruptors fertility; obesity (↑ aromatase converts T→estrogen → gynecomastia); diabetes/insulin resistance; ultraprocessed food; chronic stress; poor sleep testosterone link - Aging: ~1%/year after 30–40 in healthy men — may stay normal without hypogonadism if no comorbidities - Individual variation: 900 vs 500 both "normal" — CAG repeat length affects receptor sensitivity; symptoms matter more than number - Mortality: T <214 ng/dL → 2× mortality risk; super-physiologic (>1800) → blood thickening, cardiac fibrosis, stroke risk - Free …

concepts.title: TFCDC Vibe Coding Skills
concepts.q: What five fundamental skills does the TFCDC mnemonic (friendly cat dances constantly) capture for vibe coding?
concepts.a: Thinking (define what to build), Frameworks (steer AI to proven stacks), Checkpoints (version control), Debugging (methodical fix cycles), and Context (rich prompts, mockups, environment). Tool-agnostic across Replit, Cursor, Claude Code, etc.
concepts.definition: The five fundamental skills of vibe coding from Vibe Coding 101 with Replit (Replit × DeepLearning.AI), summarized by tina huang with the mnemonic "The friendly cat dances constantly": 1. Thinking — Thoroughly define what to build (four levels of thinking vibe coding + PRD) 2. Frameworks — Direct AI toward proven libraries and stack choices 3. Checkpoints — Version control (git/GitHub) so work is never lost 4. Debugging — Methodical find-problem → try-fix cycles; paste errors and screenshots 5. Context — Rich prompts, mockups, examples, and environment details improve output Tool-agnostic: applies whether using Replit, Windsurf, Cursor, or Claude Code.

concepts.title: THC vs CBD
concepts.q: Why is CBD not a reliable antidote if someone has taken too much THC?
concepts.a: THC is the primary psychoactive CB1 agonist; CBD is non-intoxicating with multi-mechanism effects. Blinded studies show high-dose CBD can amplify THC via hepatic CYP inhibition that slows THC metabolism, rather than canceling the high.
concepts.definition: THC (Δ9-tetrahydrocannabinol) — Primary psychoactive cannabinoid; high-affinity partial agonist at CB1. Produces intoxication, time distortion, appetite stimulation ("munchies"), memory disruption, analgesia, and biphasic anxiety effects. Very little THC needed in brain for psychoactivity. CBD (cannabidiol) — Non-intoxicating at typical doses; multi-mechanism: adenosine reuptake inhibition (sedative/anti-inflammatory — "anti-caffeine"), allosteric CB1 modulation at high concentrations, TRPV1, serotonin receptors. Not a reliable THC antidote — blinded human studies (Ryan Vandrey, Johns Hopkins) show high-dose CBD can amplify THC effects via hepatic CYP enzyme inhibition (slows THC metabolism)…

concepts.title: Theater Mistake Rule
concepts.q: If a mic fails mid-talk, why does making a big apologetic scene backfire more than a calm reset?
concepts.a: The audience often does not know an error occurred. Magnifying it makes them remember forever; calmly resetting and continuing usually means nobody remembers. Also drop perfection goals—show up with energy and value for the audience.
concepts.definition: Two linked lessons from theater and keynote speaking for handling errors and pre-talk anxiety:

concepts.title: Thesis Invalidation Pivot Rule
concepts.q: When should a startup pivot versus keep pushing through slow, hard execution?
concepts.a: Pivot when operating evidence disproves a core assumption of the business thesis. Persist when the thesis remains valid but execution is merely slower or harder than expected.
concepts.definition: Pivot when operating evidence disproves a core assumption of the business thesis; persist (push) when the thesis remains valid but execution is slower or harder than expected.

concepts.title: Think, Feel, Do Intent
concepts.q: Before a meeting, what three outcome questions prevent rambling communication?
concepts.a: Ask what you want the other person to think, feel, and do. Naming belief, emotion, and action maps definite purpose onto conversation so words have a clear job.
concepts.definition: A pre-conversation clarity habit: before any meeting or interaction, ask "What do I want this person to think, feel, and do?" Maps Napoleon Hill's principle of definite purpose to communication. Without a clear outcome, speakers ramble — they say things without knowing what their words should accomplish. Intent might be to connect, persuade, inspire, or inform; the point is naming it before opening your mouth. | Dimension | Question | | --------- | -------- | | Think | What belief or understanding should they leave with? | | Feel | What emotion should they experience? | | Do | What action or response do you want? |

concepts.title: Think Like a Teacher Not Student
concepts.q: In the AI era, how should students' role shift when homework drafts can be generated automatically?
concepts.a: Stop only executing known procedures; evaluate whether work is correct, complete, and well-reasoned. Become grader, critic, and synthesizer—especially when AI produces the first draft.
concepts.definition: Pedagogical identity shift for the AI era: school historically trained students to do homework (execute known procedures, listen, practice). The emerging requirement is to think like a teacher — evaluate whether work is correct, complete, and well-reasoned, especially when AI generates the first draft. Operationalizes grade homework not do homework with a memorable role reversal: the human becomes evaluator/critic/synthesizer, not executor. Large-scale systems must train grading and novel-problem thinking, not just procedure repetition.

concepts.title: Third Eye Center Meditation
concepts.q: Why does focusing attention at the forehead (third eye) often feel more chaotic than a body scan?
concepts.a: Third eye means prefrontal cortex, not pineal gland. Brain tissue has no sensory neurons, so there is no tactile anchor—thoughts geyser up. Body scans are sensation-heavy and less thought-dominated.
concepts.definition: "Third eye" in meditation = prefrontal cortex behind forehead — not the pineal gland (melatonin/light organ; Descartes' "seat of soul" misattribution). Brain tissue has no sensory neurons — directing attention to PFC locus means: - No tactile/proprioceptive anchor. - Thoughts, memories, emotions "geyser up" and feel disorganized. - Explains overwhelming mind-wander in classic closed-eye meditation. Contrast: Body scan or external focal point = sensation-heavy → less thought dominance. Third eye = interoceptively biased focus/refocus training.

concepts.title: 30-30-30-10 Principle
concepts.q: For a one-hour keynote, roughly how should content mix across education, entertainment, inspiration, and X-factor?
concepts.a: About 30% education, 30% entertainment, 30% inspiration (energy via voice, not only inspirational topics), and 10% unique X-factor. Over-indexing on any single pillar tends to bore, weird out, or feel inauthentic.
concepts.definition: Vinh Giang's keynote content mix formula for any talk that must hold attention for ~1 hour — derived from iterating as a professional speaker who initially failed by over-indexing on education (boring), entertainment (weird magic show), or inspiration (inauthentic). | Pillar | Share | What it is | | ------ | ----- | ---------- | | Education | 30% | Really good content — actionable ideas, frameworks, insights | | Entertainment | 30% | You are part entertainer; top-paid speakers combine strong content with high entertainment | | Inspiration | 30% | Transfer of energy/vibes via how you use your voice — not necessarily "inspirational topics"; listeners can feel inspired from neutral content if d…

concepts.title: Thoughtful network economic flow
concepts.q: After AI, how might high-trust networks of thoughtful helpers allocate opportunity better than exam ranking?
concepts.a: Networks among people who love helping and thinking hard route jobs, resources, and startup capital directly via trust (e.g., coaching friendships), bypassing bureaucratic intermediaries and central test allocation.
concepts.definition: po shen loh's thesis (as a network theorist) for 21st-century wealth after AI: instead of central exam-ranking systems allocating opportunity, build high-trust networks among people who love helping others and love thinking hard. These networks create a new economic flow system — routing resources, jobs, and startup capital directly to capable individuals rather than through bureaucratic intermediaries ("somebody professional at receiving resources"). Mechanism: cross-border friendships formed through paired coaching (expii HS tutors + MS learners) → trust at hiring time → high trust remote work arbitrage → entrepreneurial spin-outs funded through personal networks. Works in developing count…

concepts.title: Thoughtful people network motivation
concepts.q: What intrinsic motivation makes thoughtful-people networks form and create economic value?
concepts.a: Thoughtful people like being around other thoughtful people—it is fun. Helpers who think hard become trusted friends, create value, and some become entrepreneurs. Select for kindness and novel-problem thinking, not top exam percentiles.
concepts.definition: po shen loh's alternative motivation system for the AI era: thoughtful people like being around other thoughtful people — it's fun. Two people who like helping others and thinking hard quickly become trusted friends. Connect thoughtful people → they naturally find ways to create value → some become entrepreneurs solving others' pain points (and earn money doing so). This thoughtful network economic flow provides ongoing 21st-century opportunity superior to central exam-ranking allocation. Selection criterion for "great people": genuinely like other people, want to do things for others, can think about never-seen problems — not top 10% exam scores.

concepts.title: Threat hunting vs alerting
concepts.q: How does threat hunting differ from traditional security alerting?
concepts.a: Alerting is reactive like a smoke detector after something bad happened. Threat hunting is proactive like a fire marshal: assume breach, inspect before fire, and ask whether access patterns are legitimate.
concepts.definition: Two modes of cybersecurity operations: - Alerting (smoke detector): reactive — signals that something bad has already happened; SOC responds to fires - Threat hunting (fire marshal): proactive — assumes breach; inspects environment before fire to identify risk areas and anomalous sequences Threat hunting asks: does this person with these permissions have legitimate access to the data they're touching? Operates under assume breach doctrine. damien lewke positions nebulock as enabling democratized high-leverage hunting across existing tool layers — not replacing smoke detectors but adding fire marshals.

concepts.title: Three business obstacles
concepts.q: What three obstacles typically stall people before launching a six-figure lifestyle business?
concepts.a: Idea (what to sell), sales (why buy from me / fear of being sleazy), and time/energy/focus (day job constraints). Each maps to skill clusters like niche validation, magnetic marketing, and energy management.

concepts.title: Three-by-Five Strength Protocol
concepts.q: What flexible ranges define Galpin's 3-by-5 template for strength and power training?
concepts.a: 3-5 exercises, 3-5 reps, 3-5 sets, 3-5 minutes rest, 3-5 sessions per week. Strength uses about 85%+ of estimated 1RM; power uses the same structure at roughly 40-70% with emphasis on bar velocity.
concepts.definition: andy galpin's flexible template for speed, power, and strength: - 3–5 exercises per session (fewer if short on time) - 3–5 reps per set - 3–5 sets per exercise - 3–5 minutes rest between sets - 3–5 sessions per week Minimum viable: 3×3 for 3 exercises, 3×/week (~20 min). Maximum: 5×5 for 5 exercises, 5 days/week. Strength: ~85%+ of estimated 1RM. Power: same structure at ~40–70% with emphasis on bar velocity. No need to test true 1RM — use rep-max conversion charts or RPE ("could've done 1–2 more").

concepts.title: Three Core Drives
concepts.q: What three drives does Paul Conti use to model human striving?
concepts.a: Aggressive drive (assertion/competition), pleasure drive (enjoyment), and generative drive (create, understand, improve). Generative drive propels benevolent action and happiness when in balance.
concepts.definition: paul conti's model of human striving: 1. Aggressive drive — assertion, competition, self-protection; necessary but destructive when excessive (envy, destruction) or collapsed (demoralization) 2. Pleasure drive — pursuit of enjoyment; healthy in balance, pathological when it dominates 3. Generative drive — create, understand, improve (generative drive); the propellant for benevolent action and happiness

concepts.title: Three Critical Tasks Daily
concepts.q: How many cognitively demanding tasks should you put on a daily deep-focus list?
concepts.a: No more than three—sometimes one or two. Everything else (email rhythm, meetings, exercise, social life) sits outside as automaticity that should not consume deep focus allocation.
concepts.definition: Daily planning rule from a Berkeley professor (shared with Huberman during his master's): write no more than three cognitively demanding tasks per day — sometimes one or two. Everything else (email rhythm, meetings, exercise, social life) sits outside the list as automaticity — known procedures that don't require deep focus allocation.

concepts.title: Three-for-One Ratio
concepts.q: In conversation, what ratio of questions to self-stories cures main-character syndrome?
concepts.a: For every story you tell about yourself, ask three questions about the other person. Making others feel seen usually leaves a stronger impression than dominating airtime.
concepts.definition: Vinh Giang's conversation balance rule for curing main character syndrome: for every story you tell about yourself, ask three questions to the other person. Sequence: first question → second question → third question. Don't be the person who keeps talking about themselves. Be the person who makes others feel seen — they'll remember you as more interesting than anyone who dominated the airtime. Derived from Dale Carnegie via how to stop sounding unprofessional at work: the people you walk away impressed by usually asked about your life and recalled details weeks later.

concepts.title: Three Hellos Daily
concepts.q: How can daily micro-interactions reduce anxiety about meeting new people?
concepts.a: Say hello to three new people each day, escalating from a simple greeting to how-are-you, a compliment, then a small ask. Progressive low-stakes reps desensitize fear better than one heroic conversation.
concepts.definition: A daily exposure API Vinh Giang assigns for reducing anxiety when meeting new people: say hello to three new people every day, escalating complexity over time. | Level | Script | | ----- | ------ | | 1 | "Hi, good morning." — walk on | | 2 | "Hi, good morning. How are you?" | | 3 | "Hi, good morning. How are you? Beautiful shirt — I love it." | | 4 | Above + small ask: "Could you recommend a great café close by?" | Continue inventing levels. Goal: desensitize new-people anxiety through progressive micro-interactions, not one heroic conversation. Pairs with rejection desensitization — both treat social fear as trainable via repeated low-stakes reps.

concepts.title: Three Ms Framework
concepts.q: What do Mindset, Method, and Machine cover in high-level AI thinking?
concepts.a: Mindset is habits for finding leverage; Method decides what is worth automating and how much; Machine is technical implementation. It complements structure-focused models like the Four Cs.
concepts.definition: High-level AI thinking model: Mindset (habits for finding leverage), Method (deciding what's worth automating and how much), Machine (technical implementation). Complements the Four Cs which focus on AIOS structure.

concepts.title: Three Questions Before Common Ground
concepts.q: How should you respond when someone shares exciting news without one-upping them?
concepts.a: Ask three follow-up questions about their experience first, then connect your shared interest. Make it about them first; common ground second, so they feel elevated rather than diminished.
concepts.definition: Vinh Giang's fix for one-upping — the rapport-killing habit of immediately topping someone's story ("You bungee jumped? I skydived — way better"). One-upping problem: Common ground exists in theory, but the other person feels diminished ("I guess I'm not as cool as you"). Elegant alternative: When someone shares peak excitement (new jet ski, scary adventure, purchase), ask three follow-up questions about their experience first, then connect your shared interest. Jet-ski example: 1. "Have you taken it out yet?" 2. "Did you let the kids drive it?" 3. "How fast did you go?" (cheeky) Only then: "I love doing that with the kids too — I've got a boat; we should go out sometime." Rule: Make it abou…

concepts.title: Three Red Lines Housing Policy
concepts.q: What were Beijing's roughly 2020 three red lines meant to stop in the property sector?
concepts.a: Leverage rules that shut off developers' money-printer overnight, targeting overheated prices, empty homes, overbuilding in smaller cities, and extreme developer debt.
concepts.definition: Beijing's ~2020 leverage rules for property developers — graham stephan glosses them as shutting off the developer "money printer" overnight. Aimed at overheated prices, empty homes, overbuilding in smaller cities, and extreme developer debt.

concepts.title: Three Second Pause
concepts.q: Why pause about three seconds before answering a question instead of answering instantly?
concepts.a: Speed does not signal intelligence. The pause organizes thoughts, prevents rambling, builds anticipation, and conveys authority—making answers sound more composed and credible.
concepts.definition: Before answering a question, pause for three seconds. Most people rush to respond — from nerves or a mistaken belief that speed signals intelligence. It doesn't. Benefits: - Organize thoughts — buy time to structure the answer - Avoid rambling — prevent mouth-outrunning-brain - Build anticipation — silence draws listeners in - Convey authority — pause signals composure, control, thoughtfulness Live demo in trick brain speaking better instantly: instant "meaning of life" answer vs paused version citing Picasso — the paused version reads more composed, natural, and credible before a single content word lands. Distinct from delivery habits "pause more" (general white space) — this is a specific…

concepts.title: Three-stage LLM training
concepts.q: What are the three stages of modern frontier LLM training and what is each for?
concepts.a: Pre-training: next-token prediction on vast corpora. Mid-training: same algorithm but specialized (long context, high-quality sources). Post-training: SFT, DPO, RLVR, RLHF to unlock skills and polish tone—not mainly to teach new facts.

concepts.title: Thymic Involution & Immunity
concepts.q: What happens to the thymus from puberty onward, and why does that matter for immunity?
concepts.a: The thymus trains T cells but shrinks at puberty under sex hormones and cortisol (involutions). Residual adult thymus is tiny; surgical removal and repeated infection/stress without convalescence link to worse cancer/autoimmune and illness outcomes.
concepts.definition: The thymus (above heart, behind collarbone) trains T cells and secretes thymic hormones. Large in infancy (~baseball-sized relative to chest), shrinks at puberty under androgens/estrogens/progesterone/cortisol ("thymic involution") — residual fat + tiny globules in adults. Surgical thymus removal during cardiac surgery associated with higher 5-year cancer/autoimmune mortality (NEJM data cited). Post-infection atrophy: Every infection shrinks thymus temporarily; convalescence should allow regrowth — modern stress/work prevents recovery → repeated illness cycles (post-pandemic colleagues reporting 3–4 colds/winter vs one). 2026 Nature paper: Thymic score on MRI predicts cardiovascular/cancer m…

concepts.title: Thymus Aging & Immunity
concepts.q: How does aging of the thymus affect adult immune resilience?
concepts.a: Large in childhood, it involutes at puberty and by midlife is mostly fat with residual globules. Stress, vagus-linked atrophy, and repeated infections without recovery convalescence further impair T-cell training and raise illness risk.
concepts.definition: The thymus sits above the heart, trains T-cells (CD4/CD8), and secretes thymic hormones. Large in childhood (~baseball-sized), involutes at puberty under androgens/estrogens/cortisol — by 30s–50s mostly fat with residual globules. Thymic removal during cardiac surgery associated with higher 5-year cancer/autoimmune mortality (NEJM). Vagus nerve innervates thymus; stress and repeated infections cause thymic atrophy without recovery convalescence — post-pandemic recurrent illness pattern.

concepts.title: Tier-One Business Domains
concepts.q: Which seven business domains should you map before wiring an AI operating system?
concepts.a: Revenue, Customer, Calendar, Comms, Tasks, Meetings, and Knowledge. Connections across these ops/comms/data/planning buckets let the AIOS answer most questions a teammate would ask you.
concepts.definition: Seven buckets to map before wiring an AIOS — grouped as ops, comms, data, and planning: Revenue, Customer, Calendar, Comms, Tasks, Meetings, Knowledge. Each maps to tools you check monthly; connections should cover these so the AIOS can answer most questions a teammate would ask you.

concepts.title: Tightwad–Spendthrift Partner Mismatch
concepts.q: Why are tightwad-spendthrift couples especially prone to money conflict?
concepts.a: Opposites attract on the pain-of-paying scale, so savers and spenders marry more often than same-type pairs, then clash more over money. Plans need coordination; prenups and clear contracts also matter.
concepts.definition: Research-backed money personality framework from ben felix (ben felix rich is simple doac) — Carnegie Mellon / University of Michigan "pain of paying" scale: - Tightwads — distress spending even on needs - Spendthrifts — little pain; may regret later - Unconflicted — healthy balance (Steven Bartlett quiz result) Marriage finding: tightwads and spendthrifts more likely to marry each other than same-type pairs (opposites attract) → more marital conflict and lower satisfaction around money over time. Financial impact: saver with PERMA-defined goals + spendthrift spouse → harder to execute plan. Not insurmountable but needs coordination. Prenups: default = government decides asset split; creativ…

concepts.title: Time Blocking Productivity
concepts.q: How does time blocking differ from keeping a classic to-do list?
concepts.a: Assign every task to a specific calendar block under real constraints, matching task type to slot size (short gaps for shallow work; first long uninterrupted block for hardest deep work). Newport prefers this over abstract lists.
concepts.definition: Cal Newport's primary scheduling method: assign every task to a specific block on your actual calendar rather than maintaining abstract to-do lists. Grapple with real constraints — meetings, school pickup, fragmented gaps — then match task type to slot size.
